commonMistakes: Allowing knees to cave inward during the ascent
Not reaching proper depth with hips below knees
Shifting weight to toes instead of keeping it on heels
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a squat rack with safety bars set just below your lowest squat position. Avoid if you have knee or lower back injuries without proper rehabilitation and technique assessment.

exercises.name: Barbell Squat To A Bench
exercises.overview: The barbell squat to bench teaches proper squat depth and hip hinge mechanics while providing a depth reference. It's excellent for beginners learning squat technique and for experienced lifters working on consistency.
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes. To begin, first place a flat bench or a box behind you. The flat bench is used to teach you to set your hips back and to hit depth.

Then, set the bar on a rack that best matches your height. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, step under the bar and place the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) across it.
Hold on to the bar using both arms at each side and lift it off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out. Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back. This will be your starting position. (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section).

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees and sitting your hips back as you maintain a straight posture with the head up. Continue down until you slightly touch the bench behind you. Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front. If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs and extend the hips to go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Sit back onto the bench rather than straight down
Pause briefly on the bench without fully relaxing
Drive explosively off the bench using your heels
Keep tension in your muscles even when touching the bench
commonMistakes: Fully relaxing and sitting down on the bench instead of lightly touching
Using the bench to bounce back up rather than driving with legs
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the bench height allows for proper squat depth. The bench should be positioned so your hips reach parallel or slightly below when seated. Use appropriate safety measures as with regular squats.

exercises.name: Barbell Step Ups
exercises.overview: Barbell step ups are a unilateral exercise that builds single-leg strength, balance, and coordination. They effectively target the quadriceps while improving functional movement patterns used in daily activities.
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck) and stand upright behind an elevated platform (such as the one used for spotting behind a flat bench). This is your starting position.

Place the right foot on the elevated platform. Step on the platform by extending the hip and the knee of your right leg. Use the heel mainly to lift the rest of your body up and place the foot of the left leg on the platform as well. Breathe out as you execute the force required to come up.

Step down with the left leg by flexing the hip and knee of the right leg as you inhale. Return to the original standing position by placing the right foot of to next to the left foot on the initial position.
Repeat with the right leg for the recommended amount of repetitions and then perform with the left leg.
exerciseTips: Drive through the heel of the foot on the platform
Step down with control rather than jumping down
Keep your torso upright throughout the movement
Focus on the working leg doing all the work
commonMistakes: Using the bottom leg to push off and assist the movement
Leaning forward excessively during the step up
Stepping down too quickly without control
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the platform height allows your thigh to be parallel to the ground when your foot is placed on it. Use a stable, non-slip surface and consider starting with bodyweight before adding load.

exercises.name: Barbell Stiff Leg Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell stiff leg deadlift is a hip-hinge movement that primarily targets the hamstrings and glutes while strengthening the posterior chain. This exercise emphasizes the eccentric lengthening of the hamstrings.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a barbell with an overhand grip at hip level.
Keep a slight bend in your knees and maintain this angle throughout.
Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips back and lowering the barbell toward the floor.
Keep the bar close to your body and maintain a neutral spine.
Lower until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings or the bar reaches mid-shin level.
Drive your hips forward to return to the starting position.
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell close to your body throughout the movement
Focus on pushing your hips back rather than bending forward
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid rounding your back
Feel the stretch in your hamstrings at the bottom position
commonMistakes: Rounding the back instead of maintaining a neutral spine
Bending the knees too much and turning it into a squat
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back injuries. Start with lighter weight to master the hip hinge movement pattern before progressing to heavier loads.

exercises.name: Barbell Sumo Squat
exercises.overview: The barbell sumo squat is a wide-stance variation of the traditional squat that emphasizes the inner thighs and glutes while still targeting the quadriceps. The wider stance allows for a more upright torso position.
instructions: Set up a barbell on your upper back in a squat rack with feet wider than shoulder-width.
Point your toes out at approximately 45-degree angles.
Unrack the barbell and step back to clear the rack posts.
Lower your body by bending at the hips and knees, keeping knees tracking over toes.
Descend until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows.
Drive through your heels and extend hips and knees to return to the starting position.
exerciseTips: Keep your knees tracking in line with your toes throughout
Maintain an upright torso and avoid excessive forward lean
Push your knees out to engage your glutes and adductors
Focus on sitting back with your hips while descending
commonMistakes: Allowing the knees to cave inward during the movement
Taking too wide a stance which limits depth
Leaning forward excessively instead of maintaining upright posture
exercises.safetyInfo: The wide stance requires good hip and ankle mobility. Those with hip or knee issues should start with bodyweight to assess comfort and range of motion before adding load.

exercises.name: Barbell T-Bar Row
exercises.overview: The barbell T-bar row is a compound pulling exercise that effectively targets the lats and middle back. The neutral grip position often feels more natural than other rowing variations and allows for heavy loading.
instructions: Position one end of a barbell in a corner or landmine attachment and load the other end with weight plates.
Straddle the bar and bend forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and knees slightly bent.
Grasp the bar close to the weight plates with both hands or use a T-bar handle attachment.
Pull the bar up toward your lower chest, driving your elbows back and squeezing your shoulder blades.
Lower the weight with control back to the starting position, feeling a stretch in your lats.
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to protect your lower back
Drive your elbows back rather than out wide
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Maintain a neutral spine throughout the movement
commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the pull
Using the legs to help lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with lower back problems should use lighter weight or consider chest-supported alternatives. Proper hip hinge technique is crucial for safety.

exercises.name: Barbell Tempo Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell tempo deadlift emphasizes time under tension to build strength through controlled movement. The tempo component increases muscle activation and improves movement quality while building posterior chain strength.
instructions: Set up in conventional deadlift position with barbell over mid-foot.
Grip bar with hands just outside legs, maintain neutral spine.
Drive through heels to lift bar, extending hips and knees simultaneously.
At top, pause for 1 second maintaining full hip extension.
Lower bar slowly over 3 seconds, controlling the eccentric phase.
Reset at bottom and repeat for prescribed repetitions.
exerciseTips: Control the eccentric lowering phase for full 3 seconds
Maintain tension throughout the entire movement
Keep the bar close to your body during both phases
Focus on perfect form rather than heavy weight
commonMistakes: Rushing through the tempo portions
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Losing tightness during the pause at the top
exercises.safetyInfo: Use significantly lighter weight than normal deadlift due to increased time under tension. Ensure proper deadlift technique before adding tempo components.

exercises.name: Barbell Tempo Squat
exercises.overview: The tempo squat emphasizes the eccentric portion of the squat, increasing time under tension and building strength through the full range of motion. This variation improves control, stability, and strength in the stretched position.
instructions: Set up with barbell on your upper back and feet shoulder-width apart.
Begin the descent slowly, taking 3-4 seconds to reach the bottom position.
Descend until your thighs are parallel to the floor or as deep as mobility allows.
Pause briefly at the bottom position maintaining tension.
Drive through your heels to return to standing position at normal speed.
Reset your breathing and repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Count the tempo in your head to maintain consistency
Keep your core braced throughout the extended descent
Maintain control and tension even during the slow lowering phase
Focus on proper knee tracking over your toes throughout
commonMistakes: Losing tempo consistency and speeding up the descent
Relaxing at the bottom instead of maintaining muscle tension
Using too much weight and compromising the tempo
exercises.safetyInfo: Use significantly less weight than your normal squat due to the increased time under tension. Those with knee issues should be cautious with extended time in the bottom position.

exercises.name: Barbell Upright Row Wide Grip
exercises.overview: The wide grip barbell upright row targets the side delts and upper traps while building shoulder width and upper body strength. The wider grip reduces shoulder stress compared to narrow grip variations.
instructions: Stand upright holding a barbell with an overhand grip, hands positioned wider than shoulder-width apart.
Let the barbell hang in front of your body with arms fully extended and feet hip-width apart.
Pull the barbell straight up along your body by lifting your elbows high and wide.
Continue pulling until the bar reaches chest level with your elbows higher than your wrists.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Maintain control throughout the descent and repeat for desired repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep the barbell close to your body throughout the entire movement
Lead with your elbows and keep them higher than your wrists
Maintain an upright posture without leaning backward
Focus on lifting the elbows rather than the weight
commonMistakes: Using too narrow of a grip which can cause shoulder impingement
Pulling the bar too high above chest level
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or rotator cuff issues should avoid this exercise or use a wider grip. Stop immediately if you feel any shoulder pain during the movement.

exercises.name: Barbell Walking Lunge
exercises.overview: The barbell walking lunge is a dynamic compound movement that builds unilateral leg strength while challenging balance and coordination. It's excellent for developing functional strength and addressing muscle imbalances between legs.
instructions: Begin standing with your feet shoulder width apart and a barbell across your upper back.

Step forward with one leg, flexing the knees to drop your hips. Descend until your rear knee nearly touches the ground. Your posture should remain upright, and your front knee should stay above the front foot.
Drive through the heel of your lead foot and extend both knees to raise yourself back up.
Step forward with your rear foot, repeating the lunge on the opposite leg.
exerciseTips: Drive through your front heel to maximize quad activation
Keep your torso upright throughout the entire movement
Control the descent for 2-3 seconds to increase time under tension
Step far enough forward so your front shin remains vertical
commonMistakes: Allowing the front knee to cave inward
Taking steps that are too short, causing the knee to track over the toes
Leaning forward excessively during the descent
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have knee instability or ankle mobility issues. Start with bodyweight before adding a barbell to master the movement pattern.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Grip Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell wide grip deadlift increases the range of motion and emphasizes the upper back and rear delts more than conventional deadlifts. It's excellent for building pulling strength and improving posture.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot on the floor.
Bend down and grip the bar with hands much wider than shoulder-width apart.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a neutral spine.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees simultaneously.
Pull the bar straight up, keeping it close to your body throughout.
Stand tall with hips and knees fully extended, then lower with control.
exerciseTips: Keep the bar path straight and close to your body
Engage your lats to keep the bar tight
Drive your hips forward at the top
Maintain tension in your upper back throughout
commonMistakes: Gripping too wide causing shoulder strain
Allowing the bar to drift away from the body
Rounding the upper back excessively
exercises.safetyInfo: The wide grip increases range of motion and shoulder stress. Those with shoulder mobility issues or injuries should avoid this variation. Start with lighter weight than conventional deadlifts.

exercises.name: Barbell Zercher Deadlift
exercises.overview: The barbell Zercher deadlift is an advanced compound movement where the barbell is held in the crook of the elbows. This front-loaded position emphasizes the quadriceps and core while teaching an upright torso position.
instructions: Set a barbell on a rack at about elbow height or deadlift it to your elbows.
Position the bar in the crook of your elbows with arms crossed over your chest.
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart and maintain an upright torso.
Lower the weight by sitting back at the hips and bending the knees.
Descend until the bar reaches mid-shin level or as low as mobility allows.
Drive through your heels and extend hips and knees to return to standing.
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows close together and chest up throughout
Maintain an upright torso position more than in conventional deadlifts
Focus on sitting back with your hips while keeping knees tracking over toes
Use elbow sleeves or padding to protect your arms from the barbell
commonMistakes: Allowing the torso to fold forward excessively
Letting the elbows spread apart and losing bar position
Descending too quickly without control
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced movement requiring significant core strength and mobility. The bar position can be uncomfortable on the arms, so consider using padding. Avoid if you have elbow or wrist injuries.

exercises.name: Bent Over Barbell Row
exercises.overview: This fundamental pulling exercise targets the middle back muscles and improves posture. It's essential for balanced upper body development and pulling strength.
instructions: Holding a barbell with a pronated grip (palms facing down), bend your knees slightly and bring your torso forward, by bending at the waist, while keeping the back straight until it is almost parallel to the floor. Tip: Make sure that you keep the head up. The barbell should hang directly in front of you as your arms hang perpendicular to the floor and your torso. This is your starting position.

Now, while keeping the torso stationary, breathe out and lift the barbell to you. Keep the elbows close to the body and only use the forearms to hold the weight. At the top contracted position, squeeze the back muscles and hold for a brief pause.
Then inhale and slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your chest up and shoulders back throughout the movement
Pull the bar to your lower chest or upper abdomen
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
commonMistakes: Using too much momentum and swinging the torso
Pulling the bar too high toward the neck
Allowing the lower back to round excessively
exercises.safetyInfo: Maintain neutral spine alignment and avoid excessive forward lean. Those with lower back issues should consider supported variations or reduce the weight.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

exercises.name: Bent Over One-Arm Long Bar Row
exercises.overview: This landmine-style row allows for a natural pulling pattern while providing unilateral training benefits. The single-arm approach helps address muscle imbalances and provides core stability challenges.
instructions: Put weight on one of the ends of an Olympic barbell. Make sure that you either place the other end of the barbell in the corner of two walls; or put a heavy object on the ground so the barbell cannot slide backward.
Bend forward until your torso is as close to parallel with the floor as you can and keep your knees slightly bent.
Now grab the bar with one arm just behind the plates on the side where the weight was placed and put your other hand on your knee. This will be your starting position.

Pull the bar straight up with your elbow in (to maximize back stimulation) until the plates touch your lower chest. Squeeze the back muscles as you lift the weight up and hold for a second at the top of the movement. Breathe out as you lift the weight. Tip: Do not allow for any swinging of the torso. Only the arm should move.

Slowly lower the bar to the starting position getting a nice stretch on the lats. Tip: Do not let the plates touch the floor. To ensure the best range of motion, I recommend using small plates (25-lb ones) as opposed to larger plates (like 35-45lb ones).
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and switch arms.
exerciseTips: Pull elbow close to body to maximize back engagement
Squeeze shoulder blades together at top
Keep core tight to prevent torso rotation
Focus on pulling with back muscles not arms
commonMistakes: Allowing torso to twist during the movement
Pulling with arms instead of initiating with back
Using plates that are too large limiting range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure barbell is properly secured in corner or with heavy weight. Maintain neutral spine throughout to avoid lower back strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In
exercises.movementFamily: Bent Over One-Arm Long Bar Row

exercises.name: Bent Over Two-Arm Long Bar Row
exercises.overview: This bilateral landmine row provides a stable pulling pattern that's easier on the lower back than traditional bent-over rows. The angle allows for heavy loading while maintaining proper form.
instructions: Put weight on one of the ends of an Olympic barbell. Make sure that you either place the other end of the barbell in the corner of two walls; or put a heavy object on the ground so the barbell cannot slide backward.
Bend forward until your torso is as close to parallel with the floor as you can and keep your knees slightly bent.
Now grab the bar with both arms just behind the plates on the side where the weight was placed and put your other hand on your knee. This will be your starting position.

Pull the bar straight up with your elbows in (to maximize back stimulation) until the plates touch your lower chest. Squeeze the back muscles as you lift the weight up and hold for a second at the top of the movement. Breathe out as you lift the weight. Tip: Use a stirrup or double handle cable attachment by hooking it under the end of the bar.

Slowly lower the bar to the starting position getting a nice stretch on the lats. Tip: Do not let the plates touch the floor. To ensure the best range of motion, I recommend using small plates (25-lb ones) as opposed to larger plates (like 35-45lb ones).
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Use a stirrup attachment for better grip
Drive elbows back behind your torso
Keep chest up and shoulders back
Initiate pull with shoulder blade retraction
commonMistakes: Rounding the back during the movement
Pulling too high toward chest instead of lower ribcage
Using excessive body english or momentum
exercises.safetyInfo: Secure the barbell properly before beginning. Use smaller plates to achieve full range of motion and avoid lower back strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In
exercises.movementFamily: Bent Over Two-Arm Long Bar Row

exercises.name: Bent-Arm Barbell Pullover
exercises.overview: The bent-arm barbell pullover is a classic upper body exercise that targets the lats while also engaging the chest and triceps. It's excellent for improving ribcage expansion and developing the connection between chest and back muscles.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with a barbell using a shoulder grip width.
Hold the bar straight over your chest with a bend in your arms. This will be your starting position.
While keeping your arms in the bent arm position, lower the weight slowly in an arc behind your head while breathing in until you feel a stretch on the chest.
At that point, bring the barbell back to the starting position using the arc through which the weight was lowered and exhale as you perform this movement.
Hold the weight on the initial position for a second and repeat the motion for the prescribed number of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep a consistent bend in your arms throughout the movement
Lower the weight slowly in a controlled arc
Focus on feeling the stretch in your lats and chest at the bottom
Drive your elbows forward on the return movement
commonMistakes: Straightening arms completely during the movement
Lowering the weight too far and losing shoulder stability
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. The extreme range of motion can stress the shoulder joint, so start with lighter weights to assess your mobility.

exercises.name: Box Squat with Chains
exercises.overview: A powerlifting exercise that combines box squats with chain resistance to develop explosive strength and power. The chains provide variable resistance that increases as you stand up from the box.
instructions: Begin in a power rack with a box at the appropriate height behind you. Typically, you would aim for a box height that brings you to a parallel squat, but you can train higher or lower if desired.

To set up the chains, begin by looping the leader chain over the sleeves of the bar. The heavy chain should be attached using a snap hook. Adjust the length of the lead chain so that a few links are still on the floor at the top of the movement.

Begin by stepping under the bar and placing it across the back of the shoulders. Squeeze your shoulder blades together and rotate your elbows forward, attempting to bend the bar across your shoulders. Remove the bar from the rack, creating a tight arch in your lower back, and step back into position. Place your feet wider for more emphasis on the back, glutes, adductors, and hamstrings, or closer together for more quad development. Keep your head facing forward.

With your back, shoulders, and core tight, push your knees and butt out and you begin your descent. Sit back with your hips until you are seated on the box. Ideally, your shins should be perpendicular to the ground. Pause when you reach the box, and relax the hip flexors. Never bounce off of a box.
Keeping the weight on your heels and pushing your feet and knees out, drive upward off of the box as you lead the movement with your head. Continue upward, maintaining tightness head to toe.
exerciseTips: Adjust chain length so some links remain on floor at top
Pause completely on the box without relaxing
Drive explosively off the box leading with your head
Maintain tight core throughout the entire movement
commonMistakes: Chains fully lifting off ground at bottom position
Bouncing or rocking on the box
Losing upper back tightness during the pause
exercises.safetyInfo: Properly secure chains to prevent shifting during movement. Ensure box is stable and appropriate height. Start with lighter chain weight to learn the movement pattern.

exercises.name: Bradford/Rocky Presses
exercises.overview: An advanced shoulder exercise that alternates pressing in front and behind the neck. This develops shoulder strength and mobility while challenging stability throughout the full range of motion.
instructions: Sit on a Military Press Bench with a bar at shoulder level with a pronated grip (palms facing forward). Tip: Your grip should be wider than shoulder width and it should create a 90-degree angle between the forearm and the upper arm as the barbell goes down. This is your starting position.
Once you pick up the barbell with the correct grip, lift the bar up over your head by locking your arms.
Now lower the bar down to the back of the head slowly as you inhale.
Lift the bar back up to the starting position as you exhale.
Lower the bar down to the starting position slowly as you inhale. This is one repetition.
Alternate in this manner until you complete the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Maintain strict form throughout the alternating movement
Keep core engaged to protect lower back
Use lighter weight than standard overhead press
Control the bar path behind the neck carefully
commonMistakes: Using too much weight for the range of motion
Lowering bar too far behind the neck
Arching back excessively during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise requires good shoulder mobility and should be avoided by those with shoulder impingement or neck issues. Use significantly lighter weight than standard pressing movements.

exercises.name: Buffalo Bar Squat
exercises.overview: The buffalo bar squat uses a specialized barbell with curved, angled ends that sits differently on the back than a straight bar. It can be easier on the shoulders while providing a unique training stimulus.
instructions: Position the buffalo bar on your upper traps, noting the curved ends that angle downward.
Grip the bar at a comfortable width along the angled portions.
Unrack and step back into your preferred squat stance.
Descend into the squat while maintaining tight upper back tension.
Drive through your heels to return to the starting position.
Re-rack the bar carefully maintaining control throughout.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper back extremely tight to control the bar
Find your grip position on the angled portions for comfort
Maintain vertical bar path despite the bar's unique shape
Focus on keeping your elbows pulled down and back
commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to shift forward due to inadequate upper back tightness
Gripping too wide on the curved portions causing instability
exercises.safetyInfo: The buffalo bar's design can be challenging for those with shoulder mobility issues. The curved ends require strong upper back engagement to maintain proper bar position.

exercises.name: Cambered Bar Squat
exercises.overview: The cambered bar squat uses a specially designed barbell with hanging weights that oscillate during the movement. It dramatically increases the stability demands and teaches superior motor control.
instructions: Load the cambered bar and position it on your upper traps as you would a regular barbell.
The curved design will create an unstable load that moves during the lift.
Unrack carefully and stabilize the bar before stepping back.
Perform your squat while actively stabilizing the oscillating weight.
Control the bar throughout the entire range of motion.
Re-rack with extra caution due to the bar's instability.
exerciseTips: Start with significantly less weight than your regular squat
Move slowly and deliberately to control the oscillation
Keep your core extremely tight throughout the movement
Focus on smooth acceleration out of the bottom position
commonMistakes: Using too much weight before mastering the stability demands
Moving too quickly and losing control of the oscillating load
exercises.safetyInfo: The cambered bar creates significant instability and requires advanced lifting experience. Use spotters and start with very light weights to learn the movement pattern safely.

exercises.name: Chain Squat
exercises.overview: The Chain Squat is an advanced variation of the back squat that uses chains to create accommodating resistance, making the weight lighter at the bottom and heavier at the top. This technique helps develop explosive power and strength through the full range of motion.
instructions: Set up chains on both ends of the barbell so they drape over the bar and partially rest on the ground.
Position the barbell on your upper trapezius muscles and step back from the rack with feet shoulder-width apart.
Maintain proper squat form with chest up, shoulders back, and neutral spine.
Descend by pushing hips back and bending knees until hip crease is below knee level.
As you descend, more chain links will rest on the ground, decreasing the load.
Drive up explosively through your heels, with resistance increasing as chains lift off the ground.
Complete the movement by fully extending hips and knees at the top.
exerciseTips: Focus on explosive concentric movement to overcome increasing resistance
Maintain proper squat mechanics despite variable loading
Control the descent even as the load decreases
Keep your core braced throughout the variable resistance
commonMistakes: Using too much chain weight before mastering regular squats
Allowing form to break down during the variable resistance phases
Not adjusting chain length properly for your squat depth
exercises.safetyInfo: This is an advanced technique requiring mastery of regular back squats first. Ensure chains are properly secured and the correct length. Start with lighter chain weight to adapt to variable resistance. Use safety bars in a squat rack.

exercises.name: Clean and Press
exercises.overview: The clean and press combines Olympic lifting technique with strict overhead pressing strength. It develops explosive power in the clean phase followed by pure pressing strength.
instructions: Assume a shoulder-width stance, with knees inside the arms. Now while keeping the back flat, bend at the knees and hips so that you can grab the bar with the arms fully extended and a pronated grip that is slightly wider than shoulder width. Point the elbows out to sides. The bar should be close to the shins. Position the shoulders over or slightly ahead of the bar. Establish a flat back posture. This will be your starting position.

Begin to pull the bar by extending the knees. Move your hips forward and raise the shoulders at the same rate while keeping the angle of the back constant; continue to lift the bar straight up while keeping it close to your body.

As the bar passes the knee, extend at the ankles, knees, and hips forcefully, similar to a jumping motion. As you do so, continue to guide the bar with your hands, shrugging your shoulders and using the momentum from your movement to pull the bar as high as possible. The bar should travel close to your body, and you should keep your elbows out.

At maximum elevation, your feet should clear the floor and you should start to pull yourself under the bar. The mechanics of this could change slightly, depending on the weight used. You should descend into a squatting position as you pull yourself under the bar.

As the bar hits terminal height, rotate your elbows around and under the bar. Rack the bar across the front of the shoulders while keeping the torso erect and flexing the hips and knees to absorb the weight of the bar.
Stand to full height, holding the bar in the clean position.
Without moving your feet, press the bar overhead as you exhale. Lower the bar under control .
exerciseTips: Pause briefly in the clean position before pressing
Press the bar straight up without using leg drive
Keep your core tight throughout the press phase
Lower the bar under control to the front rack position
commonMistakes: Using leg drive during the press portion
Arching the back excessively during the press
Not fully extending the arms at the top
exercises.safetyInfo: Requires good shoulder mobility and stability. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or lower back issues. Use lighter weights than clean and jerk.

exercises.name: Close-Grip Barbell Bench Press
exercises.overview: A compound pressing movement that primarily targets the triceps while still engaging chest and shoulders. Excellent for building tricep mass and pressing strength.
instructions: Lie back on a flat bench. Using a close grip (around shoulder width), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in, come down slowly until you feel the bar on your middle chest. Tip: Make sure that - as opposed to a regular bench press - you keep the elbows close to the torso at all times in order to maximize triceps involvement.

After a second pause, bring the bar back to the starting position as you breathe out and push the bar using your triceps muscles. Lock your arms in the contracted position, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly again. Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
exerciseTips: Keep elbows tucked close to your sides
Lower the bar to mid-chest level
Press straight up following natural bar path
Squeeze triceps at the top
commonMistakes: Grip too narrow causing wrist strain
Elbows flaring out wide like regular bench
Touching bar too high on chest
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when working with heavy weights. Grip should be shoulder-width, not extremely narrow to avoid wrist stress.

exercises.name: Conventional Deadlift
exercises.overview: The conventional deadlift is a fundamental hip-hinge movement that develops posterior chain strength and power. It's considered one of the most effective exercises for building overall strength and muscle mass.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, toes under the barbell with the bar over mid-foot.
Bend at hips and knees to grip the bar with hands just outside your legs using an overhand or mixed grip.
Keep your chest up, shoulders back, and maintain a neutral spine with the bar close to your shins.
Drive through your heels and extend your hips and knees simultaneously to lift the bar.
Stand tall with shoulders back and hips fully extended at the top.
Lower the bar by pushing your hips back first, then bending your knees to return the bar to the floor.
exerciseTips: Keep the bar path vertical and close to your body throughout the movement
Engage your lats to prevent the bar from drifting forward
Drive through your whole foot, not just your heels
Maintain a neutral neck position by looking straight ahead
commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body during the lift
Hyperextending the back at the top of the movement
Starting with hips too low, creating an inefficient pulling position
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back injuries or disc problems. Always warm up thoroughly and start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern before progressing to heavier loads.

exercises.name: Cyclist Squat
exercises.overview: The cyclist squat uses a narrow stance and elevated heels to maximize quadriceps activation while minimizing hip involvement. It's particularly effective for building the quad strength needed for cycling and knee-dominant activities.
instructions: Set up a barbell in a squat rack at shoulder height and position it on your upper traps.
Place your feet in a narrow stance with heels elevated on weight plates, toes pointing forward.
Unrack the bar and take a step back, maintaining an upright torso position.
Descend by bending at the knees and hips, keeping your torso as vertical as possible.
Squat down until your thighs are parallel or below, then drive up through your heels to return to start.
exerciseTips: Keep your torso as upright as possible throughout the movement
Focus on driving your knees forward over your toes
Maintain tension in your core to support your spine
Control the tempo on both the descent and ascent
commonMistakes: Using too wide of a stance, reducing quad emphasis
Leaning forward excessively during the squat
Allowing knees to cave inward during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places high demands on the knees and ankles. Avoid if you have knee injuries or limited ankle mobility. Start with lighter weights to assess comfort and range of motion.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Reverse Fly
exercises.overview: The Barbell Bent Over Reverse Fly targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids and upper back muscles. This exercise helps improve posture and shoulder stability while balancing the effects of pressing movements.
instructions: Hold a barbell with an overhand grip, hands positioned wider than shoulder-width apart.
Hinge at the hips to bend forward until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor, keeping a slight bend in your knees.
Let the barbell hang at arm's length with a slight bend in your elbows.
Raise the barbell out to your sides in a wide arc until your arms are parallel to the floor.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top, then slowly lower the barbell back to the starting position.
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged to protect your lower back
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising form
Raising the torso up during the lift instead of keeping it stationary
Allowing the elbows to bend excessively during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places stress on the lower back due to the bent-over position. Those with lower back issues should consider seated or chest-supported variations. Start with light weight to master the movement pattern.

exercises.name: Barbell Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell curl is the classic bicep-building exercise that allows for progressive overload. It effectively targets both heads of the biceps for arm development.
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright while holding a barbell at a shoulder-width grip. The palm of your hands should be facing forward and the elbows should be close to the torso. This will be your starting position.
While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second and squeeze the biceps hard.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary at your sides throughout the movement
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top of each repetition
Control the weight on the way down for maximum muscle tension
Stand with feet shoulder-width apart for optimal stability
commonMistakes: Swinging the body or using momentum to lift the weight
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Not fully extending arms at the bottom of the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form. Excessive weight can lead to elbow or wrist strain.

exercises.name: Barbell Curls Lying Against An Incline
exercises.overview: This incline variation eliminates momentum and body swing, providing strict isolation of the biceps. The chest support ensures pure bicep contraction throughout the movement.
instructions: Lie against an incline bench, with your arms holding a barbell and hanging down in a horizontal line. This will be your starting position.

While keeping the upper arms stationary, curl the weight up as high as you can while squeezing the biceps. Breathe out as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Only the forearms should move. Do not swing the arms.
After a second contraction, slowly go back to the starting position as you inhale. Tip: Make sure that you go all of the way down.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the incline bench
Focus on squeezing the biceps at the peak contraction
Lower the weight slowly to fully stretch the biceps
Keep wrists straight and aligned with forearms
commonMistakes: Lifting chest off the bench to gain momentum
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
Using too much weight and sacrificing form
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise reduces cheating potential but can place stress on the biceps in the stretched position. Start with lighter weights than standing curls.
exercises.movementFamily: Barbell Curls Lying Against An Incline

exercises.name: Barbell Drag Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell drag curl is an advanced bicep exercise that eliminates shoulder involvement by keeping the elbows pulled back behind the torso. This unique movement pattern provides intense bicep activation and targets the long head of the biceps effectively.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a barbell with an underhand grip at shoulder width.
Start with the bar against your thighs and pull your elbows back slightly behind your torso.
Curl the bar up by dragging it along your torso, keeping the bar in contact with your body throughout.
Continue curling until the bar reaches your lower chest, squeezing your biceps at the top.
Slowly lower the bar back down along your torso to the starting position at your thighs.
exerciseTips: Keep the bar in constant contact with your body throughout the movement
Focus on pulling your elbows back rather than forward during the curl
Squeeze your shoulder blades together to maintain proper posture
Use a controlled tempo to maximize muscle tension and activation
commonMistakes: Allowing the bar to drift away from the body during the movement
Using too much weight and compensating with body swing
Bringing the elbows forward instead of keeping them back
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires good shoulder mobility and may not be suitable for those with shoulder impingement. Start with lighter weights to master the technique before progressing.

exercises.name: Barbell Hip Thrust Single Leg
exercises.overview: The barbell hip thrust single leg is an advanced unilateral exercise that targets each glute individually while challenging core stability. This movement helps address muscle imbalances and builds serious glute strength and power.
instructions: Position your upper back against a bench with a barbell across your hips, using a pad for comfort.
Place one foot flat on the ground while extending the other leg straight out in front of you.
Hold the barbell securely with both hands and brace your core.
Drive through your planted heel to thrust your hips up while keeping the extended leg straight.
Squeeze your glutes at the top position, creating a straight line from your knee to your shoulders.
Lower your hips back down with control and repeat for all repetitions before switching legs.
exerciseTips: Keep your extended leg straight and engaged throughout the movement
Focus on driving through your heel of the planted foot
Squeeze your glutes hard at the top of each repetition
Maintain a neutral spine by keeping your core tight
commonMistakes: Allowing the extended leg to bend or touch the ground for assistance
Hyperextending the lower back instead of focusing on hip extension
Not achieving full hip extension at the top of the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: This advanced exercise requires good hip mobility and core stability. Those with lower back issues should master bilateral hip thrusts first and use lighter weight when progressing to single leg variations.
variations: Resistance Band Single Leg Hip Thrust

exercises.name: Barbell Power Shrug
exercises.overview: The barbell power shrug is an explosive variation of the traditional shrug that develops trap power and strength. This exercise is commonly used by athletes to improve pulling power and upper back development.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a barbell with an overhand grip at arm's length.
Keep your arms straight and maintain a neutral spine with shoulders back.
Explosively shrug your shoulders straight up toward your ears as high as possible.
Focus on speed and power during the upward movement.
Control the descent back to the starting position.
Reset briefly before performing the next explosive repetition.
exerciseTips: Keep your arms completely straight throughout the movement
Focus on explosive upward movement followed by controlled descent
Shrug straight up rather than rolling shoulders forward or backward
Maintain a tight core to support your spine under load
commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders instead of shrugging straight up
Bending the arms and turning it into an upright row
Using too much weight and compromising the explosive nature
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with neck or upper back injuries should use lighter weight and focus on proper form. Avoid excessive forward head posture during the movement.

exercises.name: Barbell Preacher Curl Seated
exercises.overview: The seated barbell preacher curl isolates the biceps by eliminating momentum and stabilizing the upper arms. It's excellent for building bicep peak and strength.
instructions: Sit at a preacher bench and adjust the height so your armpits rest at the top of the pad.
Grasp the barbell with an underhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Place your upper arms flat against the preacher pad with a slight bend in your elbows at the bottom.
Curl the barbell up by contracting your biceps until your forearms are vertical.
Slowly lower the weight back to the starting position, maintaining control throughout.
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists straight and avoid letting them bend backward
Maintain contact between your upper arms and the preacher pad
Control the negative portion to maximize muscle activation
Stop just short of full elbow extension to maintain tension
commonMistakes: Fully extending the arms at the bottom, which can stress the elbow joint
Lifting the upper arms off the preacher pad during the movement
Using too much weight and compromising form or range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid locking out your elbows completely at the bottom to prevent hyperextension injury. Those with elbow issues should use lighter weight and focus on form.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell reverse curl is a variation of the traditional bicep curl using an overhand grip that primarily targets the forearm extensors and brachioradialis. It's excellent for developing balanced arm strength and improving grip strength.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a barbell with an overhand grip, hands shoulder-width apart.
Keep your elbows close to your sides and maintain an upright posture.
Curl the barbell up by flexing at the elbows while keeping your wrists straight and strong.
Lower the barbell back to the starting position with control, maintaining the overhand grip.
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists in a strong, neutral position throughout the movement
Focus on slow, controlled movements to maximize muscle activation
Maintain tension in your forearms and avoid letting the bar roll in your hands
Keep your elbows stationary and close to your torso
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and allowing the wrists to bend backward
Swinging the body or using momentum to lift the weight
Letting the elbows drift forward or away from the body
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with significantly lighter weight than regular bicep curls as this exercise places more stress on the smaller forearm muscles. Those with wrist or elbow issues should proceed cautiously.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The barbell seated calf raise specifically targets the soleus muscle, which is better activated in a seated position compared to standing calf exercises.
instructions: Place a block about 12 inches in front of a flat bench.
Sit on the bench and place the ball of your feet on the block.
Have someone place a barbell over your upper thighs about 3 inches above your knees and hold it there. This will be your starting position.
Raise up on your toes as high as possible as you squeeze the calves and as you breathe out.
After a second contraction, slowly go back to the starting position. Tip: To get maximum benefit stretch your calves as far as you can.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Place the weight on your thighs just above your knees
Rise up as high as possible on your toes
Hold the peak contraction for one second
Control the descent to get a full stretch
commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom of the movement
Not achieving full range of motion
Placing the barbell too close to the knees
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the barbell is securely positioned and have a spotter help place and remove the weight. Start with lighter loads to assess comfort.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug
exercises.overview: The barbell shrug is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the upper trapezius muscles to build neck and upper back strength.
instructions: Stand up straight with your feet at shoulder width as you hold a barbell with both hands in front of you using a pronated grip (palms facing the thighs). Tip: Your hands should be a little wider than shoulder width apart. You can use wrist wraps for this exercise for a better grip. This will be your starting position.
Raise your shoulders up as far as you can go as you breathe out and hold the contraction for a second. Tip: Refrain from trying to lift the barbell by using your biceps.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Lift your shoulders straight up toward your ears
Hold the peak contraction for one second
Keep your arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on moving only your shoulder blades
commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders instead of lifting straight up
Using the arms to lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Use wrist straps if grip strength is limiting. Avoid excessive weight that causes forward head posture or neck strain.

exercises.name: Barbell Shrug Behind The Back
exercises.overview: The behind-the-back barbell shrug targets the upper trapezius from a unique angle while minimizing upper trap dominance. This variation emphasizes the middle traps and rhomboids more than traditional front shrugs.
instructions: Stand up straight with your feet at shoulder width as you hold a barbell with both hands behind your back using a pronated grip (palms facing back). Tip: Your hands should be a little wider than shoulder width apart. You can use wrist wraps for this exercise for better grip. This will be your starting position.

Raise your shoulders up as far as you can go as you breathe out and hold the contraction for a second. Tip: Refrain from trying to lift the barbell by using your biceps. The arms should remain stretched out at all times.
Slowly return to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing shoulder blades together at the top
Hold the peak contraction for 1-2 seconds
Use a controlled tempo on both up and down phases
commonMistakes: Using biceps to lift the weight instead of traps
Rolling shoulders forward or backward during the movement
Taking too wide of a grip causing shoulder impingement
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. The behind-the-back position can stress the shoulder joint in individuals with limited shoulder mobility.

exercises.name: Barbell Side Bend
exercises.overview: The barbell side bend is an isolation exercise that targets the obliques through lateral spinal flexion. It helps develop core strength and definition in the side abdominal muscles.
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below the neck). Your feet should be shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position.

While keeping your back straight and your head up, bend only at the waist to the right as far as possible. Breathe in as you bend to the side. Then hold for a second and come back up to the starting position as you exhale. Tip: Keep the rest of the body stationary.
Now repeat the movement but bending to the left instead. Hold for a second and come back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your back straight and avoid leaning forward or backward
Bend only at the waist in a pure lateral plane
Control the movement and avoid bouncing at the bottom
Keep your head in neutral position throughout
commonMistakes: Bending forward or backward instead of purely to the side
Using momentum or bouncing at the bottom of the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise can place stress on the spine, particularly for those with lower back issues. The loaded spine in lateral flexion may aggravate disc problems.

exercises.name: Barbell Single Leg Hip Thrust
exercises.overview: The barbell single leg hip thrust is an advanced glute exercise that maximally activates the gluteus maximus while challenging stability. It's highly effective for building unilateral hip extension strength and power.
instructions: Sit with your upper back against a bench and a barbell positioned over your hips.
Plant your right foot firmly on the ground and extend your left leg straight out.
Drive through your right heel to lift your hips up, creating a straight line from knee to shoulder.
Squeeze your glutes at the top position and hold briefly.
Lower your hips with control back to the starting position.
Complete all reps on one leg before switching to the other leg.
exerciseTips: Keep your chin tucked and avoid hyperextending your neck
Focus on squeezing the glute of the working leg at the top
Maintain a straight line with your extended leg throughout
Drive through your heel rather than your toes
commonMistakes: Arching the lower back excessively at the top position
Using the extended leg to assist with the movement
Rising too high and hyperextending the spine
exercises.safetyInfo: Master the bilateral hip thrust before attempting the single leg variation. Use a pad to protect your hip bones from the barbell. Avoid if you have hip flexor tightness or lower back pain.

exercises.name: Barbell Wrist Curl
exercises.overview: The barbell wrist curl is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the wrist flexor muscles in the forearms. It's essential for developing grip strength and forearm muscular development.
instructions: Sit on a bench holding a barbell with an underhand grip, hands about shoulder-width apart.
Rest your forearms on your thighs with your wrists hanging over your knees.
Allow the barbell to roll down to your fingertips by extending your wrists.
Curl your wrists upward by flexing your wrist flexors, lifting the barbell as high as possible.
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms pressed firmly against your thighs throughout the movement
Use a full range of motion by allowing the bar to roll to your fingertips
Focus on slow, controlled movements rather than using momentum
Squeeze your forearms at the top of each repetition
commonMistakes: Lifting the forearms off the thighs and using the biceps to assist
Using too much weight and sacrificing range of motion
Performing the movement too quickly without proper control
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as the wrist flexors are small muscles that fatigue quickly. Those with wrist or forearm injuries should avoid this exercise or use extremely light resistance.

exercises.name: Car Drivers
exercises.overview: An isometric shoulder exercise that involves rotating a weight plate like a steering wheel. This builds shoulder stability and endurance while challenging grip strength.
instructions: While standing upright, hold a barbell plate in both hands at the 3 and 9 o'clock positions. Your palms should be facing each other and your arms should be extended straight out in front of you. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by rotating the plate as far to one side as possible. Use the same type of movement you would use to turn a steering wheel to one side.
Reverse the motion, turning it all the way to the opposite side.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Maintain straight arms throughout the exercise
Rotate as far as possible in each direction
Keep your core engaged for stability
Control the movement speed
commonMistakes: Bending the arms during rotation
Not rotating through full range of motion
Using too heavy a weight and compromising form
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern. Avoid jerky movements that could strain the shoulder joints.

exercises.name: Close-Grip EZ Bar Curl
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the biceps with emphasis on the inner/short head due to the close grip. The EZ bar provides a more comfortable wrist position than a straight bar.
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright while holding an E-Z Curl Bar at the closer inner handle. The palm of your hands should be facing forward and they should be slightly tilted inwards due to the shape of the bar. The elbows should be close to the torso. This will be your starting position.
While holding the upper arms stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second and squeeze the biceps hard.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Squeeze biceps hard at the top
Lower slowly with control
Focus on the inner bicep contraction
commonMistakes: Swinging body to lift weight
Moving elbows forward during curl
Using momentum instead of muscle control
exercises.safetyInfo: The EZ bar reduces wrist strain compared to straight bar. Avoid lifting too heavy which can cause elbow or shoulder stress.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: Close-Grip Standing Barbell Curl
exercises.overview: The close-grip standing barbell curl emphasizes the inner portion of the biceps and provides a different stimulus than wide-grip variations. It's excellent for building bicep peak and overall arm mass.
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands, palms up and a few inches apart.

Stand with your torso straight and your head up. Your feet should be about shoulder width and your elbows close to your torso. This will be your starting position. Tip: You will keep your upper arms and elbows stationary throughout the movement.

Curl the bar up in a semicircular motion until the forearms touch your biceps. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and contract your biceps hard for a second at the top. Tip: Avoid bending the back or using swinging motions as you lift the weight. Only the forearms should move.
Slowly go back down to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides throughout the movement
Maintain a straight torso and avoid swinging
Squeeze biceps hard at the top of each rep
Control the weight on the way down
commonMistakes: Using too wide of a grip
Swinging the body to generate momentum
Not controlling the eccentric portion
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid using excessive weight that forces you to swing or use momentum. Keep wrists in a neutral position to prevent strain.

exercises.name: Decline EZ Bar Triceps Extension
exercises.overview: The decline EZ bar triceps extension is a compound isolation exercise that effectively targets all three heads of the triceps. The EZ bar provides a more comfortable grip angle while the decline position allows for an extended range of motion.
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and slowly lay down on the bench.

Using a close grip (a grip that is slightly less than shoulder width), lift the EZ bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked and elbows in. The arms should be perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position. Tip: In order to protect your rotator cuff, it is best if you have a spotter help you lift the barbell off the rack.

As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary, bring the bar down slowly by moving your forearms in a semicircular motion towards you until you feel the bar slightly touch your forehead. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lift the bar back to the starting position by contracting the triceps and exhaling.
Repeat until the recommended amount of repetitions is performed.
exerciseTips: Use a spotter when lifting the bar off the rack for safety
Keep your elbows pointed forward and avoid letting them flare outward
Lower the bar in a controlled arc toward your forehead, not straight down
Focus on squeezing the triceps at the top of each repetition
commonMistakes: Lowering the bar too quickly without control, risking injury to the face
Using too wide of a grip which reduces triceps activation
Allowing the elbows to move backward during the lowering phase
exercises.safetyInfo: Always use a spotter when possible and ensure the bar is secured properly. The EZ bar shape reduces wrist strain compared to a straight barbell. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow pain or injuries.

exercises.name: Finger Curls
exercises.overview: Finger Curls are an isolation exercise specifically targeting the finger flexor muscles and grip strength. This exercise is beneficial for athletes requiring strong grip strength and those recovering from hand injuries.
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands and your palms facing up; hands spaced about shoulder width.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position.
Lower the bar as far as possible by extending the fingers. Allowing the bar to roll down the hands, catch the bar with the final joint in the fingers.
Now curl bar up as high as possible by closing your hands while exhaling. Hold the contraction at the top.
exerciseTips: Allow the bar to roll down to your fingertips before curling back up
Use a full range of motion from fingers extended to fully flexed
Keep your wrists stable throughout the movement
Focus on controlled movement rather than heavy weight
commonMistakes: Not allowing the bar to roll down far enough in the fingers
Using wrist movement instead of isolated finger flexion
Rushing through the movement without proper control
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as finger muscles are smaller and weaker than other muscle groups. Ensure secure grip to prevent dropping the barbell. This exercise may aggravate existing wrist or finger injuries.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: Hex Bar Shrug
exercises.overview: The hex bar shrug is an isolation exercise that targets the upper trapezius muscles using a neutral grip position. The hex bar allows for a more natural hand position and can accommodate heavier loads than dumbbell shrugs.
instructions: Stand inside a hex bar with feet hip-width apart.
Bend at the hips and knees to grasp the handles with a neutral grip.
Stand up straight with arms fully extended and shoulders in a neutral position.
Elevate your shoulders by shrugging them up toward your ears as high as possible.
Hold the contraction briefly at the top.
Lower your shoulders back to the starting position under control.
exerciseTips: Keep your arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on moving only at the shoulder joint
Squeeze your traps hard at the top of each rep
Avoid rolling your shoulders forward or backward
commonMistakes: Rolling the shoulders in a circular motion instead of straight up and down
Bending the arms and turning it into an upright row
Using momentum to swing the weight up
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most individuals. The neutral grip position may be more comfortable for those with wrist or elbow issues compared to traditional barbell shrugs.

exercises.name: Incline Barbell Triceps Extension
exercises.overview: This incline variation of triceps extensions provides a greater stretch on the triceps long head while reducing stress on the elbows compared to flat bench versions.
instructions: Hold a barbell with an overhand grip (palms down) that is a little closer together than shoulder width.
Lie back on an incline bench set at any angle between 45-75-degrees.
Bring the bar overhead with your arms extended and elbows in. The arms should be in line with the torso above the head. This will be your starting position.

Now lower the bar in a semicircular motion behind your head until your forearms touch your biceps. Inhale as you perform this movement. Tip: Keep your upper arms stationary and close to your head at all times. Only the forearms should move.
Return to the starting position as you breathe out and you contract the triceps. Hold the contraction for a second.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary throughout movement
Lower bar behind head in controlled arc
Maintain elbow position close to head
Focus on triceps contraction at top
commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out excessively
Using shoulders to assist the movement
Lowering bar too far causing shoulder strain
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when possible as the bar is positioned over your head. Start with lighter weight to assess shoulder mobility and comfort in this position.

exercises.name: Incline Bench Pull
exercises.overview: This chest-supported pulling exercise targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids and rhomboids. It's excellent for improving posture and balancing pressing movements.
instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand and lie face down on an incline bench that is set to an incline that is approximately 30 degrees.
Let the arms hang to your sides fully extended as they point to the floor.
Turn the wrists until your hands have a pronated (palms down) grip.
Now flare the elbows out. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, start to pull the dumbbells up as if you are doing a reverse bench press. You will do this by bending at the elbows and bringing the upper arms up as you let the forearms hang. Continue this motion until the upper arms are at the same level as your back. Tip: The elbows will come out to the side and your upper arms and torso should make the letter "T" at the top of the movement. Hold the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly go back down to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Squeeze shoulder blades together at top
Keep chest pressed against bench
Control the weight on descent
Focus on rear delt and rhomboid activation
commonMistakes: Using momentum to lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion
Lifting elbows too high above back level
exercises.safetyInfo: The chest-supported position makes this exercise very safe for the lower back. Ensure the bench angle allows comfortable arm movement without shoulder impingement.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

exercises.name: Lying Cambered Barbell Row
exercises.overview: A chest-supported rowing variation using a cambered bar that eliminates lower back involvement. Excellent for targeting the middle back muscles with strict form and heavy loads.
instructions: Place a cambered bar underneath an exercise bench.
Lie face down on the exercise bench and grab the bar using a palms down (pronated grip) that is wider than shoulder width. This will be your starting position.
As you exhale row the bar up as you keep the elbows close to your body to either your chest, in order to target the upper mid back, or to your stomach if targeting the lats is your goal.
After a second hold at the top, lower back down to the starting position slowly as you inhale.
exerciseTips: Keep elbows close to your body throughout the movement
Squeeze shoulder blades together at the top
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
Choose your target area - chest for upper back, stomach for lats
commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare too wide
Using the legs to help lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion at the top
exercises.safetyInfo: Ensure the cambered bar is properly secured under the bench. The prone position eliminates lower back stress but may be uncomfortable for those with breathing issues.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

exercises.name: Lying Close-Grip Barbell Triceps Extension Behind The Head
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets all three heads of the triceps by extending the forearms from an overhead position. The movement pattern emphasizes the long head of the triceps due to the overhead arm position.
instructions: While holding a barbell or EZ Curl bar with a pronated grip (palms facing forward), lie on your back on a flat bench with your head close to the end of the bench. Tip: If you are holding a barbell grab it using a shoulder-width grip and if you are using an E-Z Bar grab it on the inner handles.

Extend your arms in front of you and slowly bring the bar back in a semi circular motion (while keeping the arms extended) to a position over your head. At the end of this step your arms should be overhead and parallel to the floor. This will be your starting position. Tip: Keep your elbows in at all times.
As you inhale, lower the bar by bending at the elbows and while keeping the upper arm stationary. Keep lowering the bar until your forearms are perpendicular to the floor.

As you exhale bring the bar back up to the starting position by pushing the bar up in a semi-circular motion until the lower arms are also parallel to the floor. Contract the triceps hard at the top of the movement for a second. Tip: Again, only the forearms should move. The upper arms should remain stationary at all times.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep elbows tucked in and stationary throughout the movement
Control the descent slowly to maximize muscle tension
Focus on squeezing triceps at the top of each rep
Maintain a slight arch in your back for stability
commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out during the movement
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Lowering the bar too quickly without control
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the elbow joints. Individuals with elbow or shoulder injuries should avoid this movement or use very light weight with proper supervision.
exercises.movementFamily: Lying Close-Grip Barbell Triceps Extension Behind The Head

exercises.name: Lying High Bench Barbell Curl
exercises.overview: A bicep isolation exercise performed lying face-down on a high bench, eliminating momentum and providing constant tension throughout the range of motion.
instructions: Lie face forward on a tall flat bench while holding a barbell with a supinated grip (palms facing up). Tip: If you are holding a barbell grab it using a shoulder-width grip and if you are using an E-Z Bar grab it on the inner handles. Your upper body should be positioned in a way that the upper chest is over the end of the bench and the barbell is hanging in front of you with the arms extended and perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the elbows in and the upper arms stationary, curl the weight up in a semi-circular motion as you contract the biceps and exhale. Hold at the top of the movement for a second.
As you inhale, slowly go back to the starting position. Tip: Maintain full control of the weight at all times and avoid any swinging. Remember, only the forearms should move throughout the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms perpendicular to the floor throughout the movement
Squeeze the biceps at the top of each rep
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase slowly
Keep your wrists straight and strong
commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out to the sides
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Not maintaining full range of motion at the bottom
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter for safety. Start with lighter weight to master the position before progressing. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement.

exercises.name: Palms-Down Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise specifically targets the wrist extensors on the back of the forearms. It helps balance forearm strength and can prevent overuse injuries from gripping activities.
instructions: Start out by placing a barbell on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.
Use your arms to grab the barbell with a pronated grip (palms down) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep forearms pressed firmly against the bench throughout the movement
Control the lowering phase for 2-3 seconds to maximize muscle engagement
Focus on moving only at the wrist joint while keeping forearms stationary
Use a full range of motion from complete flexion to full extension
commonMistakes: Lifting forearms off the bench during the movement
Using momentum instead of controlled wrist extension
Gripping the bar too tightly which reduces range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have existing wrist pain or carpal tunnel syndrome. Start with light weight as wrist extensors are typically weaker than flexors.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
exercises.movementFamily: Palms-Down Wrist Curl Over A Bench

exercises.name: Palms-Up Barbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise develops the wrist flexor muscles on the underside of the forearms. It's essential for grip strength and wrist stability in pulling movements.
instructions: Start out by placing a barbell on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.
Use your arms to grab the barbell with a supinated grip (palms up) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Maintain constant contact between forearms and bench surface
Allow wrists to extend fully at the bottom for maximum stretch
Curl wrists up as high as possible while squeezing forearms
Keep grip relaxed to allow full range of motion
commonMistakes: Moving forearms during the curling motion
Not achieving full wrist extension at the bottom position
Using excessive weight that limits range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to assess wrist mobility and strength. Discontinue if you experience sharp wrist pain or numbness.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
exercises.movementFamily: Palms-Up Barbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench

exercises.name: Preacher Curl
exercises.overview: The preacher curl isolates the biceps by eliminating momentum and stabilizing the upper arms against a pad. This creates constant tension throughout the range of motion for focused bicep development.
instructions: To perform this movement you will need a preacher bench and an E-Z bar. Grab the E-Z curl bar at the close inner handle (either have someone hand you the bar which is preferable or grab the bar from the front bar rest provided by most preacher benches). The palm of your hands should be facing forward and they should be slightly tilted inwards due to the shape of the bar.
With the upper arms positioned against the preacher bench pad and the chest against it, hold the E-Z Curl Bar at shoulder length. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the bar until your upper arm is extended and the biceps is fully stretched.
As you exhale, use the biceps to curl the weight up until your biceps is fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder height. Squeeze the biceps hard and hold this position for a second.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed against the pad throughout
Control the negative portion slowly
Stop just short of full arm extension to maintain tension
Squeeze your biceps hard at the top position
commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare outward
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Fully extending arms at the bottom causing joint stress
