exercises.overview: Concentration curls are an isolation exercise that targets the biceps by eliminating momentum and body assistance. The seated position and arm bracing allows for maximum muscle isolation and peak contraction.
instructions: Sit down on a flat bench with one dumbbell in front of you between your legs. Your legs should be spread with your knees bent and feet on the floor.

Use your right arm to pick the dumbbell up. Place the back of your right upper arm on the top of your inner right thigh. Rotate the palm of your hand until it is facing forward away from your thigh. Tip: Your arm should be extended and the dumbbell should be above the floor. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Tip: At the top of the movement make sure that the little finger of your arm is higher than your thumb. This guarantees a good contraction. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in. Caution: Avoid swinging motions at any time.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions. Then repeat the movement with the left arm.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm pressed firmly against your inner thigh throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing the bicep at the top of the movement
Control the eccentric portion by lowering the weight slowly
Ensure your little finger is higher than your thumb at peak contraction
commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arm to lift off the thigh during the curl
Using momentum or swinging the weight up
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid using excessive weight that forces you to use momentum. Stop if you experience any elbow or wrist pain.

exercises.name: Cross Body Hammer Curl
exercises.overview: Cross body hammer curls target the biceps and brachialis while incorporating a crossover movement pattern. This variation enhances muscle activation through the diagonal plane of motion.
instructions: Stand up straight with a dumbbell in each hand. Your hands should be down at your side with your palms facing in.

While keeping your palms facing in and without twisting your arm, curl the dumbbell of the right arm up towards your left shoulder as you exhale. Touch the top of the dumbbell to your shoulder and hold the contraction for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbell along the same path as you inhale and then repeat the same movement for the left arm.
Continue alternating in this fashion until the recommended amount of repetitions is performed for each arm.
exerciseTips: Maintain neutral wrist position throughout the movement
Touch the dumbbell to your opposite shoulder at the peak
Keep your core engaged to prevent body sway
Alternate arms in a controlled rhythm
commonMistakes: Rotating the wrist during the curl motion
Using the torso to help lift the weight
Not bringing the weight fully across the body to the opposite shoulder
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep movements controlled to avoid shoulder impingement. Use appropriate weight to maintain proper form throughout all repetitions.

exercises.name: Decline Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.overview: The decline dumbbell triceps extension is an isolation exercise that targets all three heads of the triceps muscle. The decline angle provides a unique stimulus and allows for a greater range of motion compared to flat variations.
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and lie down with a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.

Once you are laying down, move the dumbbells in front of you at shoulder width. The palms of the hands should be facing each other and the arms should be perpendicular to the floor and fully extended. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary (and elbows in), bring the dumbbells down slowly by moving your forearms in a semicircular motion towards you until your thumbs are next to your ears. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lift the dumbbells back to the starting position by contracting the triceps and exhaling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms stationary and perpendicular to the floor throughout the movement
Control the descent to prevent the weights from dropping too quickly
Squeeze your triceps at the top of the movement for maximum contraction
Keep your elbows tucked in to maintain proper form and target the triceps effectively
commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out excessively during the movement
Moving the upper arms instead of isolating the triceps
Using momentum to lift the weights instead of controlled muscle contraction
exercises.safetyInfo: Use controlled movements to prevent dropping weights on your face or chest. Start with lighter weights to master the form before progressing to heavier loads.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Bicep Curl
exercises.overview: A fundamental bicep exercise that allows for unilateral training and incorporates wrist rotation for complete bicep development. This movement builds arm strength and size effectively.
instructions: Stand (torso upright) with a dumbbell in each hand held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso and the palms of your hand should be facing your thighs.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the right weight as you rotate the palm of the hands until they are facing forward. At this point continue contracting the biceps as you breathe out until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbell back to the starting position as your breathe in. Tip: Remember to twist the palms back to the starting position (facing your thighs) as you come down.
Repeat the movement with the left hand. This equals one repetition.
Continue alternating in this manner for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary at your sides
Rotate your wrist from neutral to supinated during the curl
Control the weight on the way down
Maintain upright posture throughout the set
commonMistakes: Swinging the weight using momentum
Moving elbows forward during the curl
Not fully supinating the wrist at the top
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the form. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow tendonitis or wrist pain.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
exercises.overview: The dumbbell bicep curl is a fundamental isolation exercise that targets the biceps muscles. It's essential for building arm strength and size while improving grip strength.
instructions: Stand up straight with a dumbbell in each hand at arm's length. Keep your elbows close to your torso and rotate the palms of your hands until they are facing forward. This will be your starting position.

Now, keeping the upper arms stationary, exhale and curl the weights while contracting your biceps. Continue to raise the weights until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a brief pause as you squeeze your biceps.
Then, inhale and slowly begin to lower the dumbbells back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary at your sides
Control the negative portion of the movement
Squeeze your biceps at the top of the curl
Maintain a straight posture throughout
commonMistakes: Swinging the body to generate momentum
Moving the elbows forward during the curl
Dropping the weight too quickly on the way down
exercises.safetyInfo: Use controlled movements to prevent bicep tendon strain. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow tendinitis or recent bicep injuries.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: Dumbbell Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The dumbbell calf raise is a simple yet effective isolation exercise for developing the calf muscles. It can be performed almost anywhere and allows for progressive overload to build lower leg strength and definition.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Position the balls of your feet on a raised surface with your heels hanging off the edge.
Lower your heels as far as possible to feel a stretch in your calves.
Rise up on your toes as high as possible, contracting your calf muscles.
Pause briefly at the top before slowly lowering back to the stretched position.
exerciseTips: Focus on the full range of motion from stretch to contraction
Pause and squeeze at the top of each rep
Control the descent to maximize the stretch
Keep your body straight and avoid bouncing
commonMistakes: Using partial range of motion
Bouncing at the bottom of the movement
Leaning forward or backward during the exercise
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with Achilles tendon issues should use caution and avoid excessive stretching. Start with body weight before adding external load.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Chest Fly
exercises.overview: The dumbbell chest fly is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the chest muscles through a wide arc of motion. It's excellent for building chest width and improving the mind-muscle connection with the pectorals.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended above your chest, palms facing each other.
Lower the dumbbells in a wide arc by slightly bending your elbows and opening your arms to the sides.
Continue lowering until you feel a stretch in your chest muscles, keeping a slight bend in your elbows throughout.
Reverse the motion by squeezing your chest muscles and bringing the dumbbells back together above your chest.
Return to the starting position with dumbbells touching lightly above your chest.
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire range of motion
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles as you bring the weights together
Control the descent to maximize the stretch in your chest
Keep your feet flat on the floor and core engaged
commonMistakes: Lowering the weights too far and straining the shoulder joint
Using too much weight and turning the movement into a press
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement or previous shoulder injuries should use caution and avoid going too deep in the stretch. Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Flyes
exercises.overview: Dumbbell flyes are an isolation exercise that targets the chest muscles through a wide arc of motion. This movement emphasizes chest muscle fiber recruitment and provides an excellent stretch.
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench with a dumbbell on each hand resting on top of your thighs. The palms of your hand will be facing each other.

Then using your thighs to help raise the dumbbells, lift the dumbbells one at a time so you can hold them in front of you at shoulder width with the palms of your hands facing each other. Raise the dumbbells up like you're pressing them, but stop and hold just before you lock out. This will be your starting position.

With a slight bend on your elbows in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon, lower your arms out at both sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, the arms should remain stationary; the movement should only occur at the shoulder joint.
Return your arms back to the starting position as you squeeze your chest muscles and breathe out. Tip: Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights.
Hold for a second at the contracted position and repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Focus on squeezing your chest at the top
Control the stretch at the bottom position
Keep your shoulder blades retracted
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and turning it into a press
Dropping the elbows too much during the movement
Allowing the shoulders to internally rotate
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the shoulder joint in a vulnerable position. Use moderate weight and avoid going too deep if you have shoulder issues.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Front Raise
exercises.overview: The dumbbell front raise is an isolation exercise that targets the front deltoids to build shoulder strength and definition. It's an excellent movement for developing anterior deltoid strength and improving shoulder stability.
instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms at your sides and palms facing your thighs.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and maintain a neutral spine with shoulders back and core engaged.
Raise one or both dumbbells forward and upward until your arms are parallel to the floor at shoulder height.
Pause briefly at the top position, feeling the contraction in your front deltoids.
Slowly lower the weights back to the starting position with control.
exerciseTips: Keep your core tight throughout the movement to prevent back arching
Control the tempo on both the lifting and lowering phases
Avoid swinging or using momentum to lift the weights
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows rather than locking them out
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with back swing
Raising the arms too high above shoulder level
Allowing the shoulders to roll forward during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have anterior shoulder impingement. Those with shoulder injuries should consult a healthcare provider before performing overhead movements.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Hammer Curl
exercises.overview: The dumbbell hammer curl targets the biceps and forearms using a neutral grip that's often more comfortable for the wrists. This variation emphasizes the brachialis muscle, helping to build overall arm thickness.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with arms at your sides and palms facing your body.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and maintain a neutral grip throughout the movement.
Curl one dumbbell up toward your shoulder by flexing your bicep while keeping your wrist straight.
Squeeze your bicep at the top of the movement, then slowly lower the dumbbell back down.
Repeat with the opposite arm, alternating between sides, or perform both arms simultaneously.
Continue for the desired number of repetitions maintaining control throughout.
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary and close to your body throughout
Maintain the neutral grip position without rotating your wrists
Focus on squeezing your biceps at the top of each rep
Control the lowering phase to maximize muscle engagement
commonMistakes: Swinging the weights or using momentum to lift heavier loads
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe for most people and may be easier on the wrists than traditional curls. Those with elbow issues should start with lighter weights.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Chest Fly
exercises.overview: The dumbbell incline chest fly is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the upper portion of the chest muscles. This movement provides an excellent stretch and contraction for chest development while improving shoulder mobility.
instructions: Set an incline bench to 30-45 degrees and lie back with a dumbbell in each hand.
Start with arms extended above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Keep your feet planted firmly on the ground and maintain a slight arch in your back.
Lower the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc until you feel a stretch in your chest.
Bring the dumbbells back together above your chest using the same arc motion.
Maintain the slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement.
exerciseTips: Keep a consistent slight bend in your elbows throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing your chest muscles at the top of the movement
Lower the weights slowly to maximize the stretch and muscle activation
Maintain tension in your chest throughout the entire range of motion
commonMistakes: Lowering the weights too far and risking shoulder injury
Turning the movement into a pressing motion by bending elbows excessively
Using weights that are too heavy for proper form
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement should avoid deep stretching at the bottom position. Start with lighter weights to master the movement pattern and avoid going below chest level.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Shoulder Raise
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the front and side deltoids while seated on an incline bench. It's effective for building shoulder definition and strength in a stable position.
instructions: Sit on an Incline Bench while holding a dumbbell on each hand on top of your thighs.

Lift your legs up to kick the weights to your shoulders and lean back. Position the dumbbells above your shoulders with your arms extended. The arms should be perpendicular to the floor with your palms facing forward and knuckles pointing towards the ceiling. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the arms straight and locked, lift the dumbbells by raising the shoulders from the bench as you breathe out.
Bring back the dumbbells to the starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your core engaged throughout the movement
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
Maintain straight arms with only a slight elbow bend
Focus on lifting with your shoulders, not your arms
commonMistakes: Using momentum to swing the weights up
Allowing the shoulders to shrug toward the ears
Bending the elbows excessively during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Start with lighter weights to assess shoulder mobility and strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: The dumbbell lateral raise is an isolation exercise that specifically targets the side deltoids to build shoulder width and definition. This movement is essential for developing the rounded, capped shoulder appearance.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with palms facing your body.
Keep a slight bend in your elbows and maintain this angle throughout the movement.
Raise the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc until they reach shoulder height.
Pause briefly at the top with arms parallel to the floor, then slowly lower the weights back down.
Control the descent and stop just short of your sides before beginning the next repetition.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions maintaining strict form throughout.
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement
Focus on leading with your pinkies as you raise the weights
Keep your torso upright and avoid swinging or using momentum
Control both the lifting and lowering phases of the movement
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and swinging the dumbbells up with momentum
Raising the weights too high above shoulder level
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder impingement should use lighter weights and avoid raising the arms above shoulder height. Stop if you feel any pinching sensation in the shoulder.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying One-Arm Rear Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise specifically targets the often-neglected rear deltoid while lying on an incline bench. It's excellent for improving shoulder balance and posture.
instructions: While holding a dumbbell in one hand, lay with your chest down on a slightly inclined (around 15 degrees when measured from the floor) adjustable bench. The other hand can be used to hold to the leg of the bench for stability.

Position the palm of the hand that is holding the dumbbell in a neutral manner (palms facing your torso) as you keep the arm extended with the elbow slightly bent. This will be your starting position.

Now raise the arm with the dumbbell to the side until your elbow is at shoulder height and your arm is roughly parallel to the floor as you exhale. Tip: Maintain your arm perpendicular to the torso while keeping your arm extended throughout the movement. Also, keep the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbell to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Squeeze your shoulder blade toward your spine at the top
Keep your arm perpendicular to your torso throughout
Focus on leading with your pinky finger
Pause briefly at the top of each repetition
commonMistakes: Using the upper back muscles instead of the rear deltoid
Allowing the arm to drift forward during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Use light weights as the rear deltoid is a small muscle. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or recent shoulder injuries.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In
exercises.movementFamily: Dumbbell Lying One-Arm Rear Lateral Raise

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying Pronation
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the forearm muscles responsible for pronation (rotating the palm downward). It's particularly useful for athletes who need strong rotational grip strength.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench face down with one arm holding a dumbbell and the other hand on top of the bench folded so that you can rest your head on it.
Bend the elbows of the arm holding the dumbbell so that it creates a 90-degree angle between the upper arm and the forearm.

Now raise the upper arm so that the forearm is perpendicular to the floor and the upper arm is perpendicular to your torso. Tip: The upper arm should be parallel to the floor and also creating a 90-degree angle with your torso. This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, externally rotate your forearm so that the dumbbell is lifted forward as you maintain the 90 degree angle bend between the upper arms and the forearm. You will continue this external rotation until the forearm is parallel to the floor. At this point you will hold the contraction for a second.
As you breathe in, slowly go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Maintain the 90-degree elbow angle throughout the movement
Rotate only through your forearm, not your entire arm
Control the weight in both directions
Keep your upper arm stationary against your torso
commonMistakes: Moving the upper arm during rotation
Using too much weight and losing control
Rotating past the natural range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as forearm muscles fatigue quickly. Stop if you experience wrist or elbow pain. This exercise is contraindicated for those with recent wrist or elbow injuries.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying Rear Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the rear deltoids using both arms simultaneously while lying on an incline bench. It's essential for balanced shoulder development and improved posture.
instructions: While holding a dumbbell in each hand, lay with your chest down on a slightly inclined (around 15 degrees when measured from the floor) adjustable bench.
Position the palms of the hands in a neutral manner (palms facing your torso) as you keep the arms extended with the elbows slightly bent. This will be your starting position.

Now raise the arms to the side until your elbows are at shoulder height and your arms are roughly parallel to the floor as you exhale. Tip: Maintain your arms perpendicular to the torso while keeping them extended throughout the movement. Also, keep the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbells to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then switch to the other arm.
exerciseTips: Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top
Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Focus on lifting with your rear delts, not your back
Control the negative portion of each rep
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with back muscles
Raising the arms too high above shoulder level
exercises.safetyInfo: Use light weights as the rear deltoid is a small muscle group. Avoid if you have shoulder impingement or recent shoulder surgery.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying Supination
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise strengthens the forearm muscles responsible for supination (rotating the palm upward). It's beneficial for grip strength and forearm balance.
instructions: Lie sideways on a flat bench with one arm holding a dumbbell and the other hand on top of the bench folded so that you can rest your head on it.
Bend the elbows of the arm holding the dumbbell so that it creates a 90-degree angle between the upper arm and the forearm.

Now raise the upper arm so that the forearm is parallel to the floor and perpendicular to your torso (Tip: So the forearm will be directly in front of you). The upper arm will be stationary by your torso and should be parallel to the floor (aligned with your torso at all times). This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, externally rotate your forearm so that the dumbbell is lifted up in a semicircle motion as you maintain the 90 degree angle bend between the upper arms and the forearm. You will continue this external rotation until the forearm is perpendicular to the floor and the torso pointing towards the ceiling. At this point you will hold the contraction for a second.
As you breathe in, slowly go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then switch to the other arm.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm stationary throughout the movement
Rotate smoothly through the full range of motion
Maintain the 90-degree angle at your elbow
Focus on controlling the weight in both directions
commonMistakes: Moving the upper arm instead of just the forearm
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Rotating beyond comfortable range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight to avoid overloading the small forearm muscles. Discontinue if you experience pain in the wrist or elbow. Not recommended for those with recent forearm or elbow injuries.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: Dumbbell One-Arm Triceps Extension
exercises.overview: The dumbbell one-arm triceps extension is an isolation exercise that targets the triceps through overhead extension, emphasizing the long head while challenging unilateral stability.
instructions: Grab a dumbbell and either sit on a military press bench or a utility bench that has a back support on it as you place the dumbbells upright on top of your thighs or stand up straight.

Clean the dumbbell up to bring it to shoulder height and then extend the arm over your head so that the whole arm is perpendicular to the floor and next to your head. The dumbbell should be on top of you. The other hand can be kept fully extended to the side, by the waist, supporting the upper arm that has the dumbbell or grabbing a fixed surface.
Rotate the wrist so that the palm of your hand is facing forward and the pinkie is facing the ceiling. This will be your starting position.
Slowly lower the dumbbell behind your head as you hold the upper arm stationary. Inhale as you perform this movement and pause when your triceps are fully stretched.
Return to the starting position by flexing your triceps as you breathe out. Tip: It is imperative that only the forearm moves. The upper arm should remain at all times stationary next to your head.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and switch arms.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm stationary throughout the movement
Lower the weight slowly to feel a stretch in the triceps
Squeeze the triceps at the top of the movement
Maintain proper shoulder blade stability
commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arm to move during the exercise
Using too heavy of a weight that compromises form
Rushing through the eccentric portion of the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Be cautious with shoulder mobility issues or elbow problems. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern and avoid dropping the weight behind your head.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: Dumbbell Prone Incline Curl
exercises.overview: The dumbbell prone incline curl is an isolation exercise that eliminates momentum through chest support, providing strict biceps training with enhanced muscle activation.
instructions: Grab a dumbbell on each hand and lie face down on an incline bench with your shoulders near top of the incline. Your knees can rest on the seat or your legs can be straddled to the sides (my preferred way).
Let your arms extend and hang naturally in front of you so that they are perpendicular to the floor.
Now keep your elbows in by your side and face the palms forward. This will be your starting position.

Raise the dumbbells by contracting the biceps until your arms are fully flexed. Exhale as you perform this portion of the movement and ensure that only the forearms move. The upper arms should remain stationary at all times.
Lower the dumbbells until your arms are fully extended.
Repeat for the recommended amount of times.
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed against the incline pad
Squeeze your biceps at the top of each rep
Lower the weights slowly to maximize muscle tension
Avoid swinging or using momentum
commonMistakes: Lifting the chest off the incline pad
Swinging the weights instead of controlled curling
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe due to the chest support eliminating momentum. Ensure proper bench angle to avoid shoulder strain.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.overview: The dumbbell pullover is a unique exercise that can target both the latissimus dorsi and chest muscles depending on technique. It's excellent for improving ribcage expansion and developing the connection between the lats and chest.
instructions: Lie perpendicular on a bench with only your upper back and shoulders supported, feet flat on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell with both hands, cupping one end of the weight against your palms.
Start with the dumbbell over your chest with arms slightly bent and maintain this arm angle throughout.
Lower the dumbbell behind your head in an arc until you feel a stretch in your lats, then return to start.
exerciseTips: Keep your hips low to increase the stretch and lat activation
Maintain the same slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement
Focus on feeling the stretch in your lats rather than just moving the weight
Control the weight on both the lowering and lifting phases
commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to bend too much and turning it into a tricep exercise
Lifting the hips too high and reducing the stretch on the target muscles
Lowering the weight too far and putting excessive stress on the shoulder joint
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with shoulder mobility issues or recent shoulder injuries should avoid this exercise. Start with lighter weight to assess comfort and range of motion before progressing.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Fly
exercises.overview: The dumbbell rear delt fly isolates the posterior deltoids while also strengthening the upper back muscles. This exercise is crucial for shoulder health and balancing the front-heavy pressing movements in most training programs.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and hinge at the hips to bend forward with a slight knee bend.
Let your arms hang straight down with palms facing each other and maintain a neutral spine.
Raise your arms out to the sides, leading with your pinkies, until they're parallel to the floor.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top and focus on contracting your rear delts.
Slowly lower the weights back to the starting position with control.
exerciseTips: Keep a slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid rounding your back
Lead with your pinkies to better target the posterior deltoids
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compensating with upper back muscles
Allowing the torso to rise during the lifting phase
Raising the arms too high above shoulder level
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise is generally safe but requires good hamstring flexibility to maintain proper position. Those with lower back issues should consider performing this seated or chest-supported.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Reverse Fly
exercises.overview: The dumbbell reverse fly is an isolation exercise that targets the posterior deltoids and upper back muscles. It's crucial for developing balanced shoulder strength and improving posture by counteracting forward shoulder position.
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip.
Hinge at the hips and lean forward until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor.
With arms slightly bent, lift the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc until they reach shoulder height.
Slowly lower the weights back to the starting position with control.
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout the entire movement
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together as you lift the weights
Keep your head in a neutral position and avoid looking up
Lead with your pinkies to better target the rear deltoids
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and allowing the arms to bend excessively
Swinging the weights up using momentum instead of muscle control
Standing too upright instead of maintaining the bent-over position
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with lower back issues should be cautious with the bent-over position. Consider performing seated or chest-supported variations if balance or back stability is a concern.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Scaption
exercises.overview: Dumbbell scaption is a corrective exercise that strengthens the rotator cuff and shoulder stabilizers by lifting weights in the scapular plane. It's essential for shoulder health and injury prevention.
instructions: This corrective exercise strengthens the muscles that stabilize your shoulder blade. Hold a light weight in each hand, hanging at your sides. Your thumbs should pointing up.
Begin the movement raising your arms out in front of you, about 30 degrees off center. Your arms should be fully extended as you perform the movement.
Continue until your arms are parallel to the ground, and then return to the starting position.
exerciseTips: Keep thumbs pointing up throughout the movement
Raise arms 30 degrees forward from your sides
Focus on squeezing shoulder blades together
Control the lowering phase slowly
commonMistakes: Raising arms directly to the sides instead of 30 degrees forward
Using momentum or swinging the weights
Lifting arms above shoulder height
exercises.safetyInfo: Use light weights to avoid shoulder impingement. Stop if you experience shoulder pain or clicking sounds.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated One-Leg Calf Raise
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the calf muscles unilaterally while seated, allowing for focused development and correction of muscle imbalances. The seated position emphasizes the soleus muscle.
instructions: Place a block on the floor about 12 inches from a flat bench.
Sit on a flat bench and place a dumbbell on your upper left thigh about 3 inches above your knee.
Now place the ball of your left foot on the block. This will be your starting position.
Raise your toes up as high as possible as you exhale and you contract your calf muscle. Hold the contraction for a second.
Slowly return to the starting position, stretching as far down as possible.
Repeat for your prescribed number of repetitions and then repeat with the right leg.
exerciseTips: Pause at the top of each rep
Lower slowly to full stretch
Keep the weight positioned above your knee
Focus on the full range of motion
commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom of the movement
Not achieving full stretch in the bottom position
Using too much weight and sacrificing range of motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Place weight securely on thigh to prevent slipping. Start with lighter weight to assess comfort and stability.
variations: Smith Machine Calf Raise Single Leg

exercises.name: Dumbbell Shrug
exercises.overview: Dumbbell shrugs target the upper trapezius muscles through shoulder elevation. This exercise builds upper back strength and improves posture.
instructions: Stand erect with a dumbbell on each hand (palms facing your torso), arms extended on the sides.

Lift the dumbbells by elevating the shoulders as high as possible while you exhale. Hold the contraction at the top for a second. Tip: The arms should remain extended at all times. Refrain from using the biceps to help lift the dumbbells. Only the shoulders should be moving up and down.
Lower the dumbbells back to the original position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Lift shoulders straight up toward ears
Squeeze traps at the top
Keep arms straight throughout
Control the descent phase
commonMistakes: Rolling shoulders forward or backward during the movement
Using momentum to bounce the weight
Bending elbows to assist the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid rolling shoulders which can cause impingement. Use controlled movements to prevent neck strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

exercises.name: Dumbbell Side Bend
exercises.overview: Dumbbell side bends target the oblique muscles through lateral spinal flexion. This exercise helps develop core strength and stability in the frontal plane.
instructions: Stand up straight while holding a dumbbell on the left hand (palms facing the torso) as you have the right hand holding your waist. Your feet should be placed at shoulder width. This will be your starting position.

While keeping your back straight and your head up, bend only at the waist to the right as far as possible. Breathe in as you bend to the side. Then hold for a second and come back up to the starting position as you exhale. Tip: Keep the rest of the body stationary.
Now repeat the movement but bending to the left instead. Hold for a second and come back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then change hands.
exerciseTips: Keep torso straight in frontal plane
Bend only at the waist laterally
Control the return to center
Keep opposite hand on hip for stability
commonMistakes: Leaning forward or backward while bending sideways
Using too heavy weight causing compensation
Bending too quickly without control
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid this exercise if you have lower back issues. Use light weight to prevent excessive lateral spine stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Single Leg Calf Raise
exercises.overview: The dumbbell single leg calf raise is a unilateral exercise that targets each calf individually while improving balance and addressing muscle imbalances. The elevated position allows for greater range of motion than floor-based calf exercises.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in one hand and stand on the edge of a step or platform with the ball of one foot on the surface.
Allow your heel to drop below the platform level to feel a stretch in your calf muscle.
Hold onto a wall or rail with your free hand for balance while keeping your working leg straight.
Push through the ball of your foot to raise your heel as high as possible, contracting your calf muscle.
Hold the peak contraction for a brief pause, then slowly lower your heel back below the platform level.
Complete all repetitions on one leg before switching to the other leg.
exerciseTips: Keep your working leg straight throughout the entire movement
Focus on getting a full stretch at the bottom and maximum contraction at the top
Control the descent to maximize muscle engagement
Maintain balance by using your free hand for light support only
commonMistakes: Bouncing at the bottom of the movement instead of controlling the stretch
Using the non-working leg to assist or provide momentum
Not achieving full range of motion by limiting the stretch or contraction
exercises.safetyInfo: Those with Achilles tendon issues or calf strains should avoid this exercise or use very light weight. Ensure you have adequate balance support to prevent falls from the elevated surface.
variations: Dumbbell Seated Single Leg Calf Raise
Smith Machine Single Leg Calf Raise

exercises.name: Dumbbell Tricep Extension -Pronated Grip
exercises.overview: The dumbbell tricep extension with pronated grip is an effective isolation exercise for building tricep strength and size. The lying position allows for better stability and control compared to standing variations.
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench holding two dumbbells directly above your shoulders. Your arms should be fully extended and form a 90 degree angle from your torso and the floor.
The palms of your hands should be facing forward, and your elbows should be tucked in. This will be your starting position.
Now, inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells until they are near your ears. Be sure to keep your upper arms stationary and your elbows tucked in.
Then, exhale and use your triceps to return the weight to the starting position.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms stationary throughout the entire movement
Focus on moving only at the elbow joint while keeping elbows tucked
Lower the weight slowly to maximize muscle tension and control
Squeeze the triceps at the top of each repetition
commonMistakes: Allowing the elbows to flare out to the sides during the movement
Moving the upper arms or shoulders instead of isolating the triceps
Lowering the weight too quickly without controlling the eccentric phase
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a spotter when possible and start with lighter weights to avoid dropping dumbbells on your face or chest. Avoid this exercise if you have elbow pain or injuries.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Tricep Extension Overhead
exercises.overview: The overhead dumbbell tricep extension isolates all three heads of the triceps through a full range of motion. It's effective for building tricep mass and strength.
instructions: Sit on a bench with back support, holding one dumbbell with both hands behind your head.
Grip the dumbbell by placing your palms against the underside of the top weight plate.
Keep your upper arms vertical and elbows pointing forward throughout the movement.
Extend your forearms upward by straightening your elbows until your arms are fully extended.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back behind your head to the starting position.
exerciseTips: Keep your elbows stationary and pointing forward throughout the movement
Lower the weight slowly to feel a stretch in your triceps
Avoid flaring your elbows out to the sides
Keep your core engaged to maintain good posture
commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out to the sides instead of keeping them forward
Using the shoulders to assist instead of isolating the triceps
Lowering the weight too quickly without controlling the negative portion
exercises.safetyInfo: Use a secure grip on the dumbbell to prevent dropping it behind your head. Those with shoulder or elbow issues should start with lighter weight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Wrist Curl
exercises.overview: Dumbbell wrist curls specifically target the wrist flexor muscles in the forearms. This isolation exercise is essential for grip strength and forearm development.
instructions: Sit on a bench holding a dumbbell in each hand with your forearms resting on your thighs.
Allow your hands to extend beyond your knees with palms facing upward.
Let the dumbbells roll down to your fingertips, allowing your wrists to extend fully.
Curl your wrists upward by flexing your wrist flexors, bringing the dumbbells back to starting position.
Repeat the movement in a controlled manner for the desired repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms pressed firmly against your thighs throughout the movement
Allow the weight to roll to your fingertips for maximum range of motion
Move only at the wrist joint, keeping your forearms stationary
Use a slow, controlled tempo to maximize muscle activation
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising range of motion
Moving the forearms instead of isolating the wrist movement
Performing the exercise too quickly without proper control
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to avoid overstraining the small wrist muscles. Those with wrist injuries should consult a healthcare provider before performing this exercise.

exercises.name: External Rotation
exercises.overview: External rotation strengthens the rotator cuff muscles, which are crucial for shoulder stability and injury prevention in overhead activities.
instructions: Lie sideways on a flat bench with one arm holding a dumbbell and the other hand on top of the bench folded so that you can rest your head on it.
Bend the elbows of the arm holding the dumbbell so that it creates a 90-degree angle between the upper arm and the forearm. Tip: Keep the arm parallel to your torso.

Now bend the elbow while keeping the upper arm stationary. In this manner, the forearm will be parallel to the floor and perpendicular to your torso (Tip: So the forearm will be directly in front of you). The upper arm will be stationary by your torso and should be parallel to the floor (aligned with your torso at all times). This will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, externally rotate your forearm so that the dumbbell is lifted up in a semicircle motion as you maintain the 90 degree angle bend between the upper arms and the forearm. You will continue this external rotation until the forearm is perpendicular to the floor and the torso pointing towards the ceiling. At this point you will hold the contraction for a second.
As you breathe in, slowly go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then switch to the other arm.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm parallel to the floor and torso
Maintain the 90-degree elbow angle throughout
Move only at the shoulder joint, keeping elbow stationary
Control the weight on both the lifting and lowering phases
commonMistakes: Allowing the elbow to drift away from the body
Using too much weight and compensating with momentum
Moving the upper arm instead of just rotating at the shoulder
exercises.safetyInfo: Use very light weight as the rotator cuff muscles are small and injury-prone. Stop if you feel pain in the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Flexor Incline Dumbbell Curls
exercises.overview: The flexor incline dumbbell curl is a bicep isolation exercise performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the long head of the biceps. The inclined position provides a greater stretch and range of motion compared to standard curls.
instructions: Hold the dumbbell towards the side farther from you so that you have more weight on the side closest to you. (This can be done for a good effect on all bicep dumbbell exercises). Now do a normal incline dumbbell curl, but keep your wrists as far back as possible so as to neutralize any stress that is placed on them.
Sit on an incline bench that is angled at 45-degrees while holding a dumbbell on each hand.

Let your arms hang down on your sides, with the elbows in, and turn the palms of your hands forward with the thumbs pointing away from the body. Tip: You will keep this hand position throughout the movement as there should not be any twisting of the hands as they come up. This will be your starting position.

Curl up the two dumbbells at the same time until your biceps are fully contracted and exhale. Tip: Do not swing the arms or use momentum. Keep a controlled motion at all times. Hold the contracted position for a second at the top.
As you inhale, slowly go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your wrists in a neutral or slightly extended position
Allow your arms to hang naturally at your sides before curling
Avoid swinging or using momentum during the movement
Squeeze the biceps hard at the top of each repetition
commonMistakes: Flexing the wrists forward which can cause strain
Starting the curl before arms are fully extended
Using the shoulders to assist with the lifting motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Keep wrists in a neutral position to avoid strain. Start with lighter weights to ensure proper form and prevent bicep or forearm injury.

exercises.name: Front Dumbbell Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the front deltoids by raising dumbbells forward. Essential for building anterior shoulder strength and definition.
instructions: Pick a couple of dumbbells and stand with a straight torso and the dumbbells on front of your thighs at arms length with the palms of the hand facing your thighs. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the left dumbbell to the front with a slight bend on the elbow and the palms of the hands always facing down. Continue to go up until you arm is slightly above parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this portion of the movement and pause for a second at the top. Inhale after the second pause.
Now lower the dumbbell back down slowly to the starting position as you simultaneously lift the right dumbbell.
Continue alternating in this fashion until all of the recommended amount of repetitions have been performed for each arm.
exerciseTips: Keep core engaged throughout movement
Control the weight on the way down
Stop at shoulder height to avoid impingement
Use a slight bend in elbows
commonMistakes: Swinging the weight up with momentum
Raising arms too high above shoulder level
Using too heavy weight causing body swing
exercises.safetyInfo: Stop if you feel shoulder impingement. Avoid lifting above shoulder height to prevent subacromial compression.

exercises.name: Front Incline Dumbbell Raise
exercises.overview: A variation of the front raise performed on an incline bench, providing better stability and isolation of the anterior deltoids. The angle changes the resistance curve for varied stimulus.
instructions: Sit down on an incline bench with the incline set anywhere between 30 to 60 degrees while holding a dumbbell on each hand. Tip: You can change the angle to hit the muscle a little differently each time.
Extend your arms straight in front of you and have your palms facing down with the dumbbells raised about 1 inch above your thighs. This will be your starting position.

Slowly raise the dumbbells straight up until they are slightly above your shoulders, while keeping your elbows locked. Squeeze at the top for a second and make sure you breathe out during this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep your head resting down against the bench and your legs on the floor at all times.
Lower the arms back to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your back pressed against the bench
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Maintain neutral wrist position
Breathe out during the lifting phase
commonMistakes: Lifting too high past shoulder level
Arching back off the bench
Using momentum instead of muscle control
exercises.safetyInfo: The incline position reduces stress on the lower back compared to standing variations. Avoid if experiencing shoulder impingement symptoms.

exercises.name: Front Two-Dumbbell Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the front deltoid muscles, essential for developing shoulder strength and definition. This movement improves overhead pressing power and shoulder stability.
instructions: Pick a couple of dumbbells and stand with a straight torso and the dumbbells on front of your thighs at arms length with the palms of the hand facing your thighs. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the dumbbells to the front with a slight bend on the elbow and the palms of the hands always facing down. Continue to go up until you arms are slightly above parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this portion of the movement and pause for a second at the top.
As you inhale, lower the dumbbells back down slowly to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Maintain slight bend in elbows throughout the movement
Control the descent to maximize muscle tension
Stop at shoulder height to avoid shoulder impingement
Keep core engaged to prevent back arching
commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging the torso to lift heavier weights
Lifting arms too high above shoulder level
Allowing wrists to bend backward under load
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have anterior shoulder impingement or rotator cuff injuries. Use lighter weights initially as the anterior deltoid is a small muscle group prone to overuse injuries.

exercises.name: Hammer Curls
exercises.overview: Hammer curls target the biceps and brachialis using a neutral grip position. This variation reduces wrist stress while emphasizing the outer bicep and forearm development.
instructions: Stand up with your torso upright and a dumbbell on each hand being held at arms length. The elbows should be close to the torso.
The palms of the hands should be facing your torso. This will be your starting position.

Now, while holding your upper arm stationary, exhale and curl the weight forward while contracting the biceps. Continue to raise the weight until the biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbell is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a brief moment as you squeeze the biceps. Tip: Focus on keeping the elbow stationary and only moving your forearm.
After the brief pause, inhale and slowly begin the lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pinned to your sides
Maintain neutral wrist position throughout
Squeeze at the top for maximum contraction
Control the eccentric lowering phase
commonMistakes: Swinging the weights using momentum
Allowing elbows to drift forward during the curl
Rotating wrists during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights to master the neutral grip position. Avoid excessive weight that forces compensation through the back or shoulders.

exercises.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
exercises.overview: The incline dumbbell curl targets the biceps from a stretched position, emphasizing the long head and providing a greater range of motion than standing curls.
instructions: Sit back on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand held at arms length. Keep your elbows close to your torso and rotate the palms of your hands until they are facing forward. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the weights forward while contracting the biceps as you breathe out. Only the forearms should move. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the dumbbells are at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second.
Slowly begin to bring the dumbbells back to starting position as your breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep upper arms stationary against the bench
Control the negative portion slowly
Squeeze biceps at the top of the movement
Don't swing or use momentum
commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms during the curl
Using too much weight and swinging
Not fully extending arms at the bottom
exercises.safetyInfo: The inclined position puts the biceps in a stretched position, increasing injury risk if proper form isn't maintained. Start with lighter weights.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: Incline Hammer Curls
exercises.overview: Incline hammer curls target the biceps and brachialis from a stretched position, emphasizing the long head of the biceps. The neutral grip also engages the brachioradialis effectively.
instructions: Seat yourself on an incline bench with a dumbbell in each hand. You should pressed firmly against he back with your feet together. Allow the dumbbells to hang straight down at your side, holding them with a neutral grip. This will be your starting position.
Initiate the movement by flexing at the elbow, attempting to keep the upper arm stationary.
Continue to the top of the movement and pause, then slowly return to the start position.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms pressed against the bench throughout the movement
Maintain a neutral wrist position with thumbs pointing up
Control the eccentric portion slowly
Avoid swinging or using momentum
commonMistakes: Allowing upper arms to move forward during the curl
Using wrists to help lift the weight instead of keeping them neutral
Rushing through the negative portion of the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: The incline position stretches the biceps significantly. Start with lighter weights than you'd use for standing curls to avoid strain.

exercises.name: Incline Inner Biceps Curl
exercises.overview: This unique bicep variation emphasizes the peak contraction of the biceps while targeting the muscle from an unusual angle. The wide arm position creates a distinctive training stimulus.
instructions: Hold a dumbbell in each hand and lie back on an incline bench.
The dumbbells should be at arm's length hanging at your sides and your palms should be facing out. This will be your starting position.

Now as you exhale curl the weight outward and up while keeping your forearms in line with your side deltoids. Continue the curl until the dumbbells are at shoulder height and to the sides of your deltoids. Tip: The end of the movement should look similar to a double biceps pose.
After a second contraction at the top of the movement, start to inhale and slowly lower the weights back to the starting position using the same path used to bring them up.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms in line with your side delts throughout the movement
Focus on squeezing the biceps at the top position
Control the weight on the way down
Maintain the wide arm position throughout the entire range of motion
commonMistakes: Allowing arms to drift inward during the curl
Using too much weight and compromising form
Not maintaining the stretched position at the bottom
exercises.safetyInfo: This exercise places significant stress on the shoulder joint. Use lighter weights and ensure proper warm-up to avoid shoulder impingement.

exercises.name: Lying Dumbbell Tricep Extension
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise effectively targets all three heads of the triceps using dumbbells for independent arm training. The lying position provides stability while allowing for a full range of motion.
instructions: Lie on a flat bench while holding two dumbbells directly in front of you. Your arms should be fully extended at a 90-degree angle from your torso and the floor. The palms should be facing in and the elbows should be tucked in. This is the starting position.
As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary with the elbows in, slowly lower the weight until the dumbbells are near your ears.
At that point, while keeping the elbows in and the upper arms stationary, use the triceps to bring the weight back up to the starting position as you breathe out.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep elbows pointing forward and tucked in
Lower dumbbells slowly until they reach ear level
Squeeze triceps hard at the top of each rep
Maintain neutral wrist position throughout
commonMistakes: Allowing elbows to flare out excessively
Lowering dumbbells past the ears creating shoulder stress
Using dumbbells that are too heavy compromising form
exercises.safetyInfo: Control the weight carefully to avoid dropping dumbbells on your face or chest. Start with lighter weight to master the movement pattern before progressing.

exercises.name: Lying One-Arm Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise for the lateral deltoid performed lying on a bench, which eliminates momentum and provides constant tension throughout the range of motion.
instructions: While holding a dumbbell in one hand, lay with your chest down on a flat bench. The other hand can be used to hold to the leg of the bench for stability.

Position the palm of the hand that is holding the dumbbell in a neutral manner (palms facing your torso) as you keep the arm extended with the elbow slightly bent. This will be your starting position.

Now raise the arm with the dumbbell to the side until your elbow is at shoulder height and your arm is roughly parallel to the floor as you exhale. Tip: Maintain your arm perpendicular to the torso while keeping your arm extended throughout the movement. Also, keep the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbell to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your arm perpendicular to your torso
Raise the dumbbell until your elbow reaches shoulder height
Maintain a slight bend in your elbow throughout
Control the lowering phase slowly
commonMistakes: Raising the arm too high above shoulder level
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Allowing the torso to rotate during the movement
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. Stop if you experience shoulder impingement pain.

exercises.name: Lying Rear Delt Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise that targets the often-neglected posterior deltoids while lying prone on a bench. This movement helps improve shoulder stability and posture by strengthening the rear delts.
instructions: While holding a dumbbell in each hand, lay with your chest down on a flat bench.
Position the palms of the hands in a neutral manner (palms facing your torso) as you keep the arms extended with the elbows slightly bent. This will be your starting position.

Now raise the arms to the side until your elbows are at shoulder height and your arms are roughly parallel to the floor as you exhale. Tip: Maintain your arms perpendicular to the torso while keeping them extended throughout the movement. Also, keep the contraction at the top for a second.
Slowly lower the dumbbells to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then switch to the other arm.
exerciseTips: Keep your chest pressed firmly against the bench
Focus on squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top
Maintain a slight bend in your elbows throughout
Control the eccentric portion of the movement
commonMistakes: Using too much weight and compromising form
Allowing the chest to lift off the bench
Turning the movement into a rowing motion
exercises.safetyInfo: Use light weights to start as the rear delts are smaller muscles prone to injury. Avoid this exercise if you have shoulder impingement issues.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

exercises.name: Lying Supine Dumbbell Curl
exercises.overview: A unique bicep exercise performed lying supine that provides constant tension on the biceps throughout the range of motion. The lying position eliminates the ability to use momentum, forcing the biceps to work harder.
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench face up while holding a dumbbell in each arm on top of your thighs.
Bring the dumbbells to the sides with the arms extended and the palms of the hands facing your thighs (neutral grip).

While keeping the arms close to your torso and elbows in, slowly lower your arms (as you keep them extended with a slight bend at the elbows) as far down towards the floor as you can go. Once you cannot go down any further, lock your upper arms in that position and that will be your starting position.

As you breathe out, slowly begin to curl the weights up as you simultaneously rotate your wrists so that the palms of the hands face up. Continue curling the weight until your biceps are fully contracted and squeeze hard at the top position for a second. Tip: Only the forearms should move. Upper arms should remain stationary and elbows should stay in throughout the movement.
Return back to the starting position very slowly.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arms stationary throughout the movement
Rotate your wrists as you curl to maximize bicep activation
Lower the weight slowly to maximize time under tension
Squeeze hard at the top of each repetition
commonMistakes: Moving the upper arms during the curl
Using momentum to swing the weights up
Not fully extending the arms at the bottom position
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with lighter weights as this position can put stress on the shoulder joint. Those with shoulder problems should avoid this exercise or use very light weights.

exercises.name: Middle Back Shrug
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the middle trapezius and rhomboids to improve posture and shoulder blade stability. Essential for counteracting forward head posture.
instructions: Lie facedown on an incline bench while holding a dumbbell in each hand. Your arms should be fully extended hanging down and pointing towards the floor. The palms of your hands should be facing each other. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold the contraction for a full second. Tip: This movement is just like the reverse action of a hug, or trying to perform rear laterals as if you had no arms.
As you inhale go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Squeeze shoulder blades together without lifting shoulders up
Hold the contraction for a full second
Keep arms straight throughout the movement
Focus on pulling shoulder blades back, not up
commonMistakes: Elevating shoulders instead of retracting shoulder blades
Using momentum instead of controlled movement
Not maintaining proper bench angle
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid excessive weight that compromises form. Keep neck in neutral position to prevent strain.
variations: Bent Over Two-Dumbbell Row With Palms In

exercises.name: One Arm Dumbbell Preacher Curl
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise that targets the biceps with strict form using a preacher bench for support. This exercise eliminates momentum and ensures focused bicep development.
instructions: Grab a dumbbell with the right arm and place the upper arm on top of the preacher bench or the incline bench. The dumbbell should be held at shoulder length. This will be your starting position.
As you breathe in, slowly lower the dumbbell until your upper arm is extended and the biceps is fully stretched.

As you exhale, use the biceps to curl the weight up until your biceps is fully contracted and the dumbbell is at shoulder height. Again, remember that to ensure full contraction you need to bring that small finger higher than the thumb.
Squeeze the biceps hard for a second at the contracted position and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exerciseTips: Position your upper arm flat against the preacher bench
Lower the weight slowly until your bicep is fully stretched
Curl with control bringing the pinky higher than thumb
Squeeze the bicep hard at the top for one second
commonMistakes: Using momentum or swinging the weight up
Not achieving full extension at the bottom position
Allowing the wrist to bend backward under load
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid locking out the elbow completely at the bottom to prevent hyperextension. Use controlled movements to prevent bicep tears.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl

exercises.name: One Arm Pronated Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.overview: This isolation exercise targets the triceps using a pronated grip, which can provide a different stimulus compared to neutral or supinated grips. It's effective for building unilateral arm strength.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell at arms length. Your arm should be perpendicular to your body. The palm of your hand should be facing towards your feet as a pronated grip is required to perform this exercise.
Place your non lifting hand on your bicep for support.
Slowly begin to lower the dumbbell down as you breathe in.
Then, begin lifting the dumbbell upward as you contract the triceps. Remember to breathe out during the concentric (lifting part of the exercise).
Repeat until you have performed your set repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exerciseTips: Keep your upper arm perpendicular to the floor throughout the movement
Use your free hand to stabilize your working arm
Lower the weight slowly to maximize muscle tension
Focus on squeezing the tricep at the top of the movement
commonMistakes: Allowing the upper arm to drift during the movement
Using momentum to lift the weight
Not achieving full range of motion at the bottom
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with light weight to master the movement pattern. The pronated grip can place additional stress on the wrist and elbow joints.
exercises.movementFamily: One Arm Pronated Dumbbell Triceps Extension

exercises.name: One Arm Supinated Dumbbell Triceps Extension
exercises.overview: The supinated grip variation of the triceps extension provides a unique stimulus to the triceps muscles. This unilateral exercise helps identify and correct strength imbalances between arms.
instructions: Lie flat on a bench while holding a dumbbell at arms length. Your arm should be perpendicular to your body. The palm of your hand should be facing towards your face as a supinated grip is required to perform this exercise.
Place your non lifting hand on your bicep for support.
Slowly begin to lower the dumbbell down as you breathe in.
Then, begin lifting the dumbbell upward as you contract the triceps. Remember to breathe out during the concentric (lifting part of the exercise).
Repeat until you have performed your set repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
Switch arms again and repeat the movement.
exerciseTips: Maintain the supinated grip throughout the entire range of motion
Keep your upper arm stationary and perpendicular to the floor
Control the eccentric portion of the lift
Stabilize your working arm with your free hand
commonMistakes: Allowing the grip to rotate during the movement
Moving the upper arm instead of just the forearm
Rushing through the eccentric phase
exercises.safetyInfo: The supinated grip may feel awkward initially and can stress the wrist. Use lighter weight until comfortable with the grip position.
exercises.movementFamily: One Arm Supinated Dumbbell Triceps Extension

exercises.name: One-Arm Flat Bench Dumbbell Flye
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting chest muscle development through a wide arc of motion. The unilateral version helps address strength imbalances between sides.
instructions: Lie down on a flat bench with a dumbbell in one hand resting on top of your thigh. The palm of your hand with the dumbbell in it should be at a neutral grip.

By using your thighs to help you get the dumbbell up, clean the dumbbell so that you can hold it in front of you with your lifting arm being fully extended. Remember to maintain a neutral grip with this exercise. Your non lifting hand should be to the side holding the flat bench for better support. This will be your starting position.

Your arm with the weight should have a slight bend on your elbow in order to prevent stress at the biceps tendon. Begin by lowering your arm with the weight in it out in a wide arc until you feel a stretch on your chest. Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement. Tip: Keep in mind that throughout the movement, your lifting arm should remain stationary; the movement should only occur at the shoulder joint.
Return your lifting arm back to the starting position as you squeeze your chest muscles and breathe out. Tip: Make sure to use the same arc of motion used to lower the weights.
Hold for a second at the contracted position and repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exerciseTips: Maintain a slight bend in your elbow throughout
Focus on the stretch at the bottom of the movement
Squeeze your chest muscle at the top
Use the same arc path up and down
commonMistakes: Bending the elbow too much turning it into a press
Lowering the weight too far causing shoulder strain
Using too heavy weight compromising form
exercises.safetyInfo: Use lighter weight than pressing movements. Stop if you feel shoulder impingement. This exercise puts significant stress on the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: One-Arm Incline Lateral Raise
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise for the lateral deltoid performed on an incline bench. The position provides stability while targeting shoulder width and definition.
instructions: Lie down sideways on an incline bench press with a dumbbell in the hand. Make sure the shoulder is pressing against the incline bench and the arm is lying across your body with the palm around your navel.
Hold a dumbbell in your uppermost arm while keeping it extended in front of you parallel to the floor. This is your starting position.

While keeping the dumbbell parallel to the floor at all times, perform a lateral raise. Your arm should travel straight up until it is pointing at the ceiling. Tip: Exhale as you perform this movement. Hold the dumbbell in the position and feel the contraction in the shoulders for a second.
While inhaling lower the weight across your body back into the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the movement.
exerciseTips: Keep the dumbbell parallel to the floor
Raise your arm straight up toward the ceiling
Squeeze your shoulder at the top
Control the descent across your body
commonMistakes: Allowing the dumbbell to tilt during movement
Using momentum to swing the weight up
Not maintaining contact with the incline bench
exercises.safetyInfo: Use appropriate weight to maintain control throughout the range of motion. Stop if you experience shoulder impingement.

exercises.name: One-Arm Side Laterals
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the lateral deltoid with added stability challenge from the supported unilateral position. The leaning position allows for greater range of motion.
instructions: Pick a dumbbell and place it in one of your hands. Your non lifting hand should be used to grab something steady such as an incline bench press. Lean towards your lifting arm and away from the hand that is gripping the incline bench as this will allow you to keep your balance.
Stand with a straight torso and have the dumbbell by your side at arm's length with the palm of the hand facing you. This will be your starting position.

While maintaining the torso stationary (no swinging), lift the dumbbell to your side with a slight bend on the elbow and your hand slightly tilted forward as if pouring water in a glass. Continue to go up until you arm is parallel to the floor. Exhale as you execute this movement and pause for a second at the top.
Lower the dumbbell back down slowly to the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Switch arms and repeat the exercise.
exerciseTips: Lead with your pinky finger
Stop at shoulder height
Control the lowering phase
Keep slight bend in elbow
commonMistakes: Raising arm too high above shoulder
Using momentum or body swing
Allowing shoulder to roll forward
exercises.safetyInfo: Avoid if you have shoulder impingement. Use lighter weights than bilateral version. Stop if you feel pinching in the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.overview: An isolation exercise targeting the wrist extensor muscles to improve grip strength and forearm balance. Essential for preventing wrist injuries and improving grip endurance.
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.

Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells with a pronated grip (palms facing down) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Keep your forearms flat against the bench throughout
Move only at the wrist joint
Control the eccentric lowering phase
Use a full range of motion from flexion to extension
commonMistakes: Using momentum to lift the weight
Lifting the forearms off the bench
Using weight that's too heavy for proper form
exercises.safetyInfo: Start with very light weight as wrist extensors are typically weak. Those with wrist injuries should avoid or modify this exercise.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
exercises.movementFamily: Palms-Down Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench

exercises.name: Palms-Up Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench
exercises.overview: This dumbbell variation allows for unilateral training of wrist flexors, helping identify and correct strength imbalances between arms while improving grip strength.
instructions: Start out by placing two dumbbells on one side of a flat bench.
Kneel down on both of your knees so that your body is facing the flat bench.
Use your arms to grab both of the dumbbells with a supinated grip (palms up) and bring them up so that your forearms are resting against the flat bench. Your wrists should be hanging over the edge.
Start out by curling your wrist upwards and exhaling.
Slowly lower your wrists back down to the starting position while inhaling. Make sure to inhale during this part of the exercise.
Your forearms should be stationary as your wrist is the only movement needed to perform this exercise.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
exerciseTips: Work one arm at a time to identify and correct strength imbalances
Keep the non-working hand on the bench for stability
Focus on slow, controlled movements in both directions
Allow dumbbells to roll slightly into fingers at bottom for deeper stretch
commonMistakes: Rushing through the movement without proper control
Not maintaining forearm contact with the bench
Using dumbbells that are too heavy for proper form
exercises.safetyInfo: The unilateral nature allows you to address imbalances but start conservatively. Stop if you experience tingling or sharp pain in the wrists.
variations: Seated One-Arm Dumbbell Palms-Down Wrist Curl
exercises.movementFamily: Palms-Up Dumbbell Wrist Curl Over A Bench

exercises.name: Power Partials
exercises.overview: Power partials are a variation of lateral raises focusing on the most challenging portion of the range of motion. They effectively target the lateral deltoids for shoulder width and strength.
